entity_name: U.S. Government Critical Minerals Funding List (2023-2026)
canonical_name: Critical Minerals Institute Funding Master Table
facts: The U.S. government has moved from policy statements to capital deployment using multiple federal pipes to accelerate domestic and allied supply chains for critical minerals and downstream manufacturing.

Funding flows through five key U.S. government divisions: DOE (notably LPO), DoD (including DPA Title III and Office of Strategic Capital), Commerce (CHIPS Program Office), USDA Rural Development, and DFC, plus EXIM as a major financing engine.
The list was compiled by the Critical Minerals Institute (CMI) and publishes exclusively on InvestorNews.com to serve as a living reference for investors, industry participants, and policymakers.
6K Additive, Inc. is a private company that received a DoD DPAI award of US$23.4M announced on Dec 5, 2023.
The DoD award expands domestic capability to upcycle waste/scrap into higher-grade metals for defense and commercial supply chains.
Frank Roberts serves as CEO & Managing Director of 6K Additive.
6K Additive produces high-performance metal powders for additive manufacturing and alloy additions using UniMelt® technology from sustainable sources.
The company targets applications in aerospace, defense, medical, automotive, and industrial markets.

The award explicitly supports turning domestic scrap into high-grade titanium, niobium, nickel, and tungsten production capacity for U.S. defense applications including aircraft structures, turbine blades, rocket engines, and radar.
Albemarle Corporation is a public company with multiple U.S. government funding awards.
Albemarle received a DOE selection/award of US$67.1M under the Battery Materials Processing/Battery Manufacturing program announced on Sep 20, 2024.
The DOE award supports an anode-materials initiative in North Carolina for lithium metal anode (LMA) materials to increase battery energy density.
Albemarle received a DoD DPA Title III award of US$90M announced on Sep 12, 2023 to expand U.S. lithium production and domestic battery supply chains.
The DoD award is tied to restarting the Kings Mountain, North Carolina mine and funding a fleet of mining equipment as hard-capex.
Albemarle received a DOE grant of US$150M announced on Oct 19, 2022 tied to its Kings Mountain, North Carolina lithium facility supporting the domestic EV supply chain.
Kent Masters serves as CEO & Chairman of Albemarle.
Albemarle is a global leader in transforming lithium and bromine into critical ingredients for mobility, energy, connectivity, and health.
Altona Rare Earths Plc is a public company receiving USTDA support for the Monte Muambe Rare Earths Project with a grant agreement in preparation and undisclosed amount.
USTDA confirmed its intention to support the project on Feb 9, 2026, following confirmation delivered Feb 8, 2026 in Cape Town.
Cédric Simonet serves as CEO & Director of Altona Rare Earths.
The Monte Muambe Project in northwest Mozambique hosts rare earths, fluorspar, and gallium mineralisation.
Altona completed over 7,800 metres of drilling and delivered a maiden JORC Mineral Resource Estimate of 13.6 Mt at 2.42% total rare earth oxides.
The company secured a 25-year mining licence in December 2024 and published a Competent Person Report and scoping study in October 2023.
Altona targets 50,000 tonnes per annum of high-grade acid-grade fluorspar production over a minimum 12-year mine life.
Gallium recovery is planned as a by-product of fluorspar tailings.
The USTDA confirmation aligned with the 2026 Critical Minerals Ministerial and the launch of FORGE to strengthen resilient critical minerals supply chains.
Amaero Ltd. is a public company that signed an EXIM credit agreement (loan) of US$22.8M announced on Feb 26, 2025.
The financing supports U.S. expansion and capacity buildout.
Hank J. Holland serves as CEO & Executive Chairman of Amaero.
Amaero has manufacturing and corporate headquarters in Tennessee, USA.
The company produces high-value C103, refractory alloy, and titanium powders for additive and advanced manufacturing for defense, space, and aviation industries.
Amaero uses advanced gas atomization technology with an industry-leading yield of AM powder.
The company is a leader in PM-HIP manufacturing of large, near-net-shape powder metallurgy parts.
The facility was originally announced as US$23.5M but decreased to US$22.8M after EXIM reduced certain capitalized fees.
American Battery Technology Company (ABAT) is a public company that received an EXIM Letter of Interest (non-binding) for up to US$900M announced on Apr 29, 2025.
The LOI supports financing tied to the Tonopah Flats Lithium Project in Nevada.
Ryan Melsert serves as CEO & Director of ABAT.
ABTC is headquartered in Reno, Nevada and pioneers technologies for domestically manufactured and recycled battery metals for EV, stationary storage, and consumer electronics.
Tonopah Flats encompasses one of the largest known measured/indicated/inferred lithium claystone resource deposits in the U.S.
Proven & Probable reserves are estimated at ~559,848 metric tonnes at an average grade of 805 ppm lithium.
American Rare Earths Limited is a public company that received an EXIM Letter of Interest (non-binding) for up to US$456M announced on Sep 24, 2024.
The LOI covers the construction/execution phase of the Cowboy State Mine area at the Halleck Creek Project with a referenced 15-year repayment term under EXIM’s Make More in America initiative.
Mark Wall serves as CEO & Executive Director of American Rare Earths.
The company advances the Halleck Creek Project in Wyoming through its wholly owned subsidiary, Wyoming Rare (USA) Inc.
Halleck Creek is a world-class rare earth deposit with plans for onsite mineral processing and separation facilities to reduce U.S. reliance on imports from China.
American Rare Earths collaborates with U.S. Government-supported R&D programs to develop innovative extraction and processing technologies for rare earth elements.
Anovion Technologies is a private company that received a DOE BIL grant of US$117M under the Battery Materials Processing & Manufacturing program announced on Oct 19, 2022.
The grant supports construction of a large-scale factory in Bainbridge, Georgia and investment in a relocated Center of Excellence in Sanborn, New York.
Anovion targets roughly 150,000 tpa of anode-grade synthetic graphite capacity by 2030.
Anovion also received an EXIM LOI for up to US$400M announced on Nov 7, 2024 for direct project financing.
Clay Kiefaber serves as CEO & Advisory Board Member of Anovion.
Anovion is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois and brings over 140 years of experience in synthetic graphite materials.
Anovion’s products were the first made in North America to gain qualification for EV applications with commercial production commencing in early 2021.
In October 2025, the Trump DOE confirmed canceling more than US$700M of earlier clean-manufacturing grants including Anovion’s, though only about US$13.8M had been disbursed.
The company continued planning its Georgia facility and broader expansion despite the cancellation.

Ares Strategic Mining Inc. is a public company awarded a DoD DLA IDIQ contract for acid-grade fluorspar with an award date of Dec 29, 2025 (posted Jan 13, 2026) and company announcement on Jan 20, 2026.
The reported award is US$168,938,267 with a stated contract ceiling up to US$250M under the five-year framework.
James Walker serves as President, CEO & Director of Ares Strategic Mining.
The contract ties to Award ID SP8000-26-D-0002 (DoD/DLA, DCSO Fort Belvoir).
Ascend Elements, Inc. is a private company that received a DOE BIL cost-share grant of ~US$316M for the Apex 1 pCAM/CAM facility in Hopkinsville, Kentucky announced on Oct 19, 2022.
Ascend Elements also received a DOE MESC Advanced Graphite Recycling cost-share grant of US$125M with Orbia’s Koura business announced on Sep 23, 2024.
Linh Austin serves as President, CEO & Director of Ascend Elements.

The company is headquartered in Westborough, Massachusetts and uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode process to convert end-of-life batteries into pCAM, cathode active materials, and battery-grade lithium carbonate.
More than US$200M had already been disbursed when the DOE moved to rescind the remaining funds in the 2025 review.
Ascend plans to complete the Kentucky plant using a mix of equity, project finance, municipal bonds, and other debt.
Critical Metals Corp is a public company that received an EXIM Letter of Interest (non-binding) for up to US$120M with a referenced 15-year term announced on Jun 16, 2025.
Tony Sage serves as CEO & Executive Chairman of Critical Metals Corp.
The company’s flagship Tanbreez project is one of the world’s largest rare earth deposits located in Southern Greenland.
Critical Metals also owns the Wolfsberg Lithium Project in Carinthia, Austria, which is the first fully permitted mine in Europe.
Reuters described the EXIM LOI as the Trump administration’s first overseas mining investment.
EnergySource Minerals LLC is a private company that received a DOE LPO conditional commitment (direct loan) of up to US$1.36B announced on Jan 15, 2025.
The borrower is SPV ESM ATLiS LLC with a structure of US$1.22B principal plus US$141M capitalized interest.
Eric Spomer serves as President & CEO of EnergySource Minerals.
Proceeds will finance construction/equipping/operation of a lithium hydroxide facility in Imperial County, California producing lithium from geothermal brine via direct lithium extraction (DLE).
DOE flags potential co-products including zinc and manganese.
Project ATLiS is expected to be the first facility in the U.S. to use DLE at commercial scale as a closed-loop power-plant-to-lithium system.
Fireweed Metals Corp is a public company that received a DoD DPA Title III award of US$15.8M announced on Dec 13, 2024.
Ian Gibbs serves as President, CEO & Director of Fireweed Metals.
The award advances the 100%-owned Mactung tungsten project toward a final investment decision.
source_title: Follow the Money: The U.S. Government Funding Hit List for Critical Minerals Companies (2023–2026) - InvestorNews

facts: Battery manufacturers in the USA currently employ 34,891 workers across 292 companies, generating combined sales of $52 billion.
The domestic battery manufacturing sector demonstrates concentrated regional production, with 32% of operations located in the South.
Electric vehicle market projections indicate sales increases exceeding 35% in 2023, creating expanded demand for domestic battery production capabilities.
The U.S. represents approximately 6% of global battery manufacturing capacity, indicating significant potential for domestic production expansion.
East Penn Manufacturing began operations in 1946 from a single-room creamery facility in Bowers, Pennsylvania, initially producing five automotive battery configurations.
East Penn Manufacturing has developed into a major battery manufacturing operation with 10,500 employees and 515 distinct product designs.
East Penn operates the globally largest single-site lead battery manufacturing facility.
The company has 7,995 employees based in Pennsylvania facilities.
East Penn’s manufacturing process achieves a 99% material recycling rate.
East Penn’s automotive battery production line has received Underwriters Laboratories validation for 98% recycled material content allocation.
East Penn Manufacturing focuses on the Deka brand, combining the founders’ names “DeLight” and “Karl”.
East Penn is the second-largest Starting, Lighting and Ignition (SLI) battery supplier in North America.
East Penn introduced the Deka Ready Power product family at ProMat 2025 in Chicago.
East Penn achieved certification as the first U.S. lead battery manufacturer to receive UL1973 validation for Reserve Power battery offerings.
The Material Handling Equipment Distributors Association (MHEDA) presented East Penn with the MVS (Most Valuable Supplier) Award for 2024 performance.

Michael Guilfoy, Director of Cell Manufacturing Operations at Tesla, acknowledged that Tesla’s Cell Manufacturing team in Texas reached a huge milestone at the end of 2024 by becoming the lowest cost per kWh battery cell producer for Tesla.
Tesla operates as both a battery manufacturer and consumer, implementing a vertically integrated approach to battery production and deployment.

Tesla’s domestic battery production centers on Gigafactory Nevada, which produces billions of cells annually and ranks among the world’s highest volume plants for electric motors, energy storage products, and vehicle powertrains.
Gigafactory Nevada is undergoing a $3.6 billion expansion that includes a new 100 GWh 4680 cell factory and Tesla’s first high-volume Semi truck manufacturing line.
Tesla’s lithium supply chain strategy involves multiple suppliers including Ganfeng Lithium, Arcadium Lithium, and Sichuan Yahua Industrial Group.
Tesla is developing lithium refining capabilities with a new facility in Texas designed to produce 50 GWh of battery-grade lithium annually.
Tesla implements Nickel-Cobalt-Aluminum (NCA) chemistry for premium models like the Model S and Model X, developed with Panasonic.
Tesla’s standard-range vehicles utilize Lithium-Iron-Phosphate (LFP) batteries, which eliminate cobalt and nickel requirements.
Tesla established a dedicated LFP battery facility near Gigafactory Nevada specifically for Megapack production.
Tesla’s Powerwall home energy storage device has been in production at Gigafactory Nevada since January 2016.
Tesla secured a $4.3 billion contract with LG Energy Solution for U.S.-built LFP batteries in July 2024, commencing in August 2027.
Tesla is developing four new versions of its 4680 battery cells for introduction in 2026, including a specialized variant code-named NC05 for robotaxi applications.
Tesla reportedly loses 70-80% of cathodes in test production for the dry-coating process compared to conventional battery makers’ 2% loss rate.
American Battery Factory (ABF) addresses a critical gap in domestic battery manufacturing through its focus on Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) cell production.
ABF was established in 2021 with headquarters in American Fork, Utah.
ABF’s primary manufacturing development centers on the planned largest U.S. LFP battery cell gigafactory for Tucson, Arizona.
The ABF Tucson facility requires a capital investment of $1.20 billion and projects employment for 1,000 workers across 267 acres.
ABF leadership includes Chairman and CEO Zhenfang “Jim” Ge, President John Kem (former Army Major General), and CFO Tyler Hortin.
China currently controls approximately 77% of global lithium cell production capacity.
ABF prismatic Lithium Iron Phosphate batteries offer cell longevity exceeding 8,000 life cycles with a projected lifespan of 20-30 years.
ABF has established an 18 GWh lithium iron phosphate battery cell offtake agreement with Lion Energy.
ABF signed a seven-year Memorandum of Understanding with Tinci Materials Texas LLC in October 2024 to secure battery chemical materials supply.
Construction on the ABF Arizona gigafactory began in October 2023, with initial production phases scheduled for 2025 completion.
EnerSys operates as an industrial technology company specializing in stored energy solutions, headquartered in Reading, Pennsylvania.
EnerSys’s 2025 restructuring includes closure of its Monterrey, Mexico plant with production transfer to Richmond, Kentucky, alongside a $4.50 million expansion.
EnerSys received selection for a $199.00 million award negotiation to develop a lithium-ion cell gigafactory.
Recent workforce adjustments at EnerSys affected approximately 575 employees, representing 11% of non-production global staff.
Stryten Energy operates from Alpharetta, Georgia, maintaining 11 U.S. manufacturing plants and battery component facilities with 2,500 employees nationwide.
Stryten Energy’s current production capacity reaches 14 gigawatt-hours annually, with expansion plans targeting increases to 24 gigawatts.
Stryten’s vertically integrated supply chain provides battery components to both internal production lines and external U.S. battery manufacturers.
Stryten announced 10 gigawatts of additional U.S. energy storage manufacturing capacity for 2025, creating manufacturing jobs across existing facilities.

The company established Storion Energy LLC through affiliate Stryten Critical E-Storage LLC, partnering with Largo Inc. to address domestic sourcing of price-competitive electrolytes for vanadium redox flow battery production.

Large Power is a global leader in custom-engineered lithium battery packs, headquartered in Guangdong, China, serving U.S.-based OEMs in industrial automation, medical devices, aerospace, and marine applications.
In 2025, Large Power expanded its U.S.-based engineering support team to provide enhanced, real-time collaboration with American OEMs on complex electrification projects.
Batteries America operates as a specialized manufacturer serving niche market segments within the domestic battery industry, founded in 1990 and headquartered in Sauk City, Wisconsin.
Ultium Cells operates as a joint venture between General Motors and LG Energy Solution, focusing on domestic battery cell production for zero-emissions vehicles.
Ultium Cells maintains production facilities in Warren, Ohio and Spring Hill, Tennessee, with a third Michigan location under development.
The combined facility investment for Ultium Cells totals $2.50 billion, generating an estimated 6,000 construction positions and 5,100 permanent operations roles.
Ultium Cells produces large-format pouch cells using nickel-cobalt-manganese-aluminum (NCMA) chemistry.
Ultium Cells announced commercial LMR prismatic cell production beginning in 2028.
The Ohio manufacturing plant reached production with output projections indicating 100 million battery cells for 2024.
U.S. Battery has maintained continuous deep cycle battery production since 1926, establishing manufacturing operations across Corona, California, Evans, Georgia, and Augusta, Georgia.
U.S. Battery reported annual revenue reached USD 128.20 million in 2025.

24M Technologies addresses fundamental limitations in lithium-ion battery manufacturing through proprietary process innovations developed from MIT research, founded in 2010 and headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Total funding for 24M Technologies reaches $253.83 million across multiple rounds, with strategic investments from KYOCERA, Asahi Kasei, and Mitsui O.S.K. Lines.
The company’s SemiSolid manufacturing platform reduces conventional lithium-ion battery production steps by 50%, achieving cost reductions up to 40%.

American Battery Solutions (ABS) addresses underserved transportation market segments through specialized battery systems, founded in 2019 and operating manufacturing facilities in Lake Orion, Michigan and Springboro, Ohio.
ABS was acquired by Komatsu America Corp. in December 2023, establishing the company as Komatsu’s battery technology center of excellence while maintaining independent operations.
The organization employs over 300 workers with collective industry experience exceeding 340 years in high-voltage battery production.
ABS secured a $132 million contract with TICO for Proliance battery systems in electric terminal tractors during April 2025.
Clarios operates as a global leader in advanced low-voltage battery manufacturing, producing over 150 million batteries annually that power one-third of vehicles worldwide.
The company operates 16 manufacturing facilities throughout the United States, employing more than 5,500 domestic workers.
Clarios announced a $6 billion investment program spanning ten years to strengthen American manufacturing capabilities in 2025.
The investment strategy allocates $2.5 billion for advanced battery expansion, $1.9 billion for critical minerals processing, and $600 million for facility modernization.
Clarios plans to establish a Manufacturing Technology & Training Center focused on workforce development and manufacturing innovation.

SK Battery America represents foreign investment in domestic battery manufacturing, establishing state-of-the-art manufacturing facilities in Commerce, Georgia and Spring Hill, Tennessee through USD 2.60 billion in capital investment.

facts: Hyundai calls its first big electric car factory in Georgia the “Metaplant.”
The Metaplant complex covers 16 million square feet and is wired for virtual reality so executives can fix the production line from Seoul using goggles.
When finished, the Metaplant will produce 300,000 vehicles a year.
The first vehicles at the Metaplant are due to roll off the line before Election Day.
Savannah residents are intrigued by the prospect of 8,500 well-paid manufacturing jobs at the plant but regard the EV product with suspicion.
A handyman named Jerold Griffiths believes that if Trump retakes the White House, the plant will convert to gas cars.
A poll found that 55 percent of Republicans say there is “zero chance” they will buy an EV, compared with only 14 percent of Democrats.
Republican voters in Savannah doubt EV cost, range, and whether charging infrastructure can match gas stations.
The presidential election has become a de facto EV referendum.
President Joe Biden staked his legacy on battery cars, now held by Vice President Kamala Harris.
The Inflation Reduction Act committed $40.2 billion in grants and rebates to encourage businesses to build and Americans to buy EVs, according to Atlas Public Policy.
Former President Donald Trump disparages EVs as weaker than traditional cars and called them a “crazy electric Band-Aid” at the Republican nomination.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp vowed to make Georgia an “electric mobility capital.”
Republican legislators in Georgia distrust EVs and have erected barriers for citizens wanting to own one.
Savannah economic boosters want to celebrate landing the Hyundai factory, but locals are angry about increased traffic and housing shortages caused by construction.
A “battery belt” of massive EV and battery investment stretches from Georgia to Michigan.
Toyota has a mammoth battery complex in North Carolina.
Ford has huge campuses in Tennessee and Kentucky.
There are three big EV/battery factories in Georgia.
Battery component makers and recyclers are creating decent-paying jobs in rural outposts across the South.
Billions of federal dollars from a Democratic president have landed mostly in rural and red congressional districts.
Georgia officials and voters like the prosperity and jobs from EVs but dislike California-style mandates and associate EVs with Democrats’ woke ideology.
Stan Cross, director of electric transportation at Southern Alliance for Clean Energy, noted the tension between wanting jobs/investment benefits without celebrating the end product.
Don Francis, founder of the EV Club of the South, owns a red Tesla and blue Nissan Leaf and flies a Trump flag outside his suburban Atlanta home.
In 1996, Francis persuaded Lenox Square Mall to install a charging station behind Bloomingdale’s.
California had ordered that 10 percent of cars sold in the state run on batteries, prompting GM to roll out the EV1.
Georgia Power named Francis head of its EV charging infrastructure and he gave VIP test drives around downtown Atlanta during the Olympics year.
In 2003, California’s mandate collapsed amid automaker opposition and a lawsuit supported by President George W. Bush’s administration.
GM recalled Georgia Power’s fleet of 50 or so EV1s and crushed them into scrap.
Francis tore out the charging stations at Lenox Square, leaving no EVs on Georgia roads for five years.
In 2009, Elon Musk’s Tesla Roadsters forced automakers to refocus on EVs out of fear of being left behind.
Francis took an early buyout from Georgia Power and became executive director of Clean Cities Georgia, channeling federal EV funding into the state’s electric fleets.
Francis worked as a rep for ClipperCreek, one of the first charging companies.
Francis mentored Aaron Luque, co-founder of EV-charging startup EnviroSpark Energy Solutions, which received a $50 million funding round.
In 2015, Atlantans bought 21,000 EVs, placing Georgia second behind California in adoption.
An obscure state tax credit created in the late 1990s to clear Atlanta’s smoggy air escalated to $5,000, the most generous in the country.
Nissan offered a two-year lease for a new Leaf for about $5,000, equal to the tax credit.
Republican state representative Chuck Martin led efforts to kill the tax credit, calling it “too open-ended” and saying taxpayers were paying for someone else’s third car.
In 2015, the Legislature lowered the tax credit to zero and slapped a $200 registration fee on EVs because plug-ins don’t pay gasoline taxes for road maintenance.
The $200 fee is the highest in the nation according to North Carolina State University’s college of engineering.
EV sales in Georgia plummeted by 80 percent after the credit was killed.
Last year, while offering billions in state subsidies to woo EV factories, the Legislature placed an unusual tax on public EV charging.
Francis criticized battling the Legislature over disincentives while the state tries to attract EV manufacturers to Georgia.
Industrial plants and jobs have been a bipartisan goal in Georgia since the 1930s, pitching cheap land, weak labor unions, and low-wage workers.
James Cobb, professor emeritus of history at the University of Georgia in Athens, noted this industrial development mania is bipartisan across Southern states.
Neighboring Tennessee and Alabama landed Volkswagen, General Motors, and Honda plants that crank out hundreds of thousands of vehicles a year.
Georgia’s only big auto success was a Kia plant opened in 2010.
In his 2023 inaugural speech for his second term, Governor Kemp announced Georgia would become “the electric mobility capital of America.”
Pat Wilson, state director of economic development, said Kemp’s interest sparked before the pandemic during a trip to Stuttgart, Germany, touring Mercedes and Porsche factories.
German executives focused on long-term electrification plans; both have U.S. headquarters in Atlanta but do not make vehicles in Georgia.
Kemp attended the groundbreaking for a $1.7 billion SK On battery factory in Commerce (an hour northeast of Atlanta) just after taking office in 2019, wooed by predecessor Nathan Deal.
The Commerce plant was the first EV/battery plant in the South.
In early 2021, GM said it would go all-electric by 2035.
In May 2021, Hyundai announced a huge EV and battery factory near Savannah to produce 300,000 vehicles a year.
Seven months later, Rivian announced it would produce 400,000 electric trucks annually at a center about 40 miles east of Atlanta.
In December 2022, Hyundai partnered with SK to raise a second, larger battery factory in Bartow County (west of Atlanta).
The three projects together are plowing more than $17.5 billion into Georgia’s economy.
State and local agencies have given more than $1 billion in support for these projects, not including tax breaks.
The state economic development department credited the growth to “Georgia’s prime location, major ports system and extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and pro-business climate.”
Automakers are building EV/battery plants due to a global consensus on converting the vehicle fleet to electric to fight climate change.
Kemp and state government were silent on the climate message; Brian Robinson noted Kemp wants economic development and jobs, not saving the planet.
In late 2022, Congress passed the Inflation Reduction Act without a single Republican vote.
The act provided generous tax credits for American factories to produce batteries/components and created incentives for a $7,500 consumer tax credit on EVs favoring domestic production.
Battery maker Freyr halted construction in Norway and moved a $1.7 billion battery cell plant to Georgia.
A Hyundai division raised a factory for electrical components ($926 million).
Electric air-travel startup Archer announced its first commercial plant ($118 million).
Chemical companies Solvay (Belgium) and Orbia (Mexico) debuted a factory for EV-battery binders and coatings ($850 million).
All four projects arrived in November.
Atlas Public Policy data shows Georgia bests all other states with a projected $24.6 billion of investment and nearly 28,000 jobs.
97 percent of this investment is arriving in Republican districts, according to Atlas’ analysis.

U.S. Representative Buddy Carter (district encompassing the Hyundai plant outside Savannah) said he is excited about EVs but not government mandates, noting Biden administration emissions rules effectively require more EVs.
Outside Cartersville, the 300-acre battery plant planned by Hyundai and SK is under construction and slated to open in late 2025.
Steve Taylor, Bartow’s sole county commissioner and Trump supporter, said they are a Republican county that embraced the EV industry because of good-paying jobs.
Battery product tax credits from the Inflation Reduction Act could remove hundreds of millions of dollars from Hyundai’s U.S. tax burden each year.
Taylor does not see Trump pulling support for the factory.
Chris Benson is city administrator of Pembroke (pop. 2,500), the nearest settlement to the Hyundai plant.
Hyundai promised to hire 8,500 workers; even hiring every resident of Pembroke for three shifts would not be enough.
Seven residential tracts are planned that would quadruple Pembroke’s population to 10,000.
Benson is planning sewers, sidewalks, and annexation of county land for the influx.
The real estate market in Pembroke is warping; properties selling for millions now instead of hundreds of thousands.
Rising property taxes may force locals off their land.
Elizabeth Norman, a Pembroke resident renovating a downtown theater, plans to launch a $4 million fundraising campaign but worries about noise and traffic from construction trucks.
In Bryan County (where Pembroke sits), only about one in every 200 vehicles is an EV.
The nearest public charging station to Pembroke is 17 miles away.
Hyundai is preparing to also make gas-electric hybrids at the plant due to slowing American EV sales.
Norman thinks the grid isn’t ready and local/state government sold residents out.
Hugh “Trip” Tollison, CEO of Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA), calls the Hyundai plant a “Super Bowl project.”
Local officials identified the site near Pembroke in 2014; a joint-development authority bought it in 2021; Hyundai came calling in 2022.
Tollison highlighted 17 suppliers announcing plans for factories (advanced electronics to headrests), promising $2.7 billion investment and almost 7,000 new jobs.
Hyundai’s pay is projected at $58,000 a year, roughly a quarter more than the region’s average.
Dan Windley, 41, a Savannah resident who upholsters private jets at Gulfstream (Savannah’s largest employer), would consider leaving for Hyundai if wages and benefits are better.
Windley is unaware Biden supported EVs but thinks he jacked up gas prices; he does not like either candidate.
Trump said at a June Detroit event that the “electric vehicle mandate will totally obliterate the U.S. auto industry.”
Trump inaccurately referred to charging times at a Florida rally.
At a New Jersey rally in May, Trump said EVs are too expensive, will be made in China, and don’t go far.
Francis likes Trump’s desire to pump natural gas and hates propping up Middle East oil regimes.
Francis worries about inflation and border issues; he considers Biden’s pause on LNG export terminal approvals an affront to American independence.
Trump said at a Georgia rally last year: “The first day in office, I will end all of that” regarding EVs.
In late July in Minnesota, Trump said: “I love electric cars. Elon Musk endorsed me.”
Francis thinks Trump got bad advice on EVs but hopes he learns his lesson.
source_title: Electric vehicles are changing Georgia’s economy. But not voters’ minds. - E&E News by POLITICO

facts: Syensqo is located at 4500 McGinnis Ferry Road, Alpharetta, GA 30005-3914 US.
The company contact number is 770-772-8200.

Syensqo, previously part of the Solvay Group, is a global leader in advanced lightweight composite and polymer materials serving the aerospace, automotive, electronics, and high-performance industries.
The company has a heritage of 160 years of technological innovation combining material design and process engineering expertise.
Syensqo's comprehensive product portfolio includes thermoset and thermoplastic prepregs, adhesives, surfacing films, resin and compound systems, carbon fibers, and tooling solutions.
The company is committed to driving innovation and sustainability by developing next-generation materials that optimize efficiency and reduce environmental impact.
Syensqo supplies adhesive bonding, automotive body panels & substructures, aerospace fuselage/wing, carbon fiber composite, epoxy, glass fiber, and other composites materials.
Syensqo introduces orange-colored grades for critical EV components using fiberglass-reinforced Amodel PPA HFFR-4133 OR resin to meet e-mobility high-voltage safety standards.
Solvay releases SolvaLite grade to mitigate BEV thermal runaway, a fast-curing epoxy prepreg that outperforms aluminum and discontinuous fiber composites in UL 2596 flammability tests.
Solvay launches Xencor XTreme for battery thermal runaway protection, a long glass fiber-reinforced PPA solution produced with partially bio-based content.
Solvay features Xydar polymer for high-heat EV battery module insulation, specifically the Xydar LCP G-330 HH glass-filled liquid crystal polymer grade.
Syensqo supports Vertical Aerospace VX4 industrialization plan through an advanced composite supply agreement to advance safer, cleaner, and scalable electric air mobility.
Vertical Aerospace achieves two-way piloted transition flight for its eVTOL prototype, with a planned commercial aircraft launch in 2028.
Vertical announces Valo commercial eVTOL aircraft with 2028 EIS plans, built on the VX4 prototype and supported by companies like Syensqo.
Syensqo and Bell join forces to industrialize composite part manufacture, leveraging Syensqo’s DDF technology and Cycom EP2750 fast-cure prepreg.
Solvay becomes Beta Technologies material supplier for AAM, providing lightweight composite materials for electric CTOL and VTOL aircraft.
AZL announces follow-up projects on electromobility casings and thermal runaway, encouraging interested companies to join consortia before a Sept. 11th kick-off.
Syensqo Cycom EP2190 epoxy prepreg has been added to the NCAMP database to lower adoption barriers and shorten customer design timelines for aerospace, defense, and AAM qualification.
Four Syensqo innovations are recognized at the 2025 SPE Automotive Awards for fiber-reinforced polymers, specialty compounds, and bio-based PPA materials.
Mercedes-AMG Petronas F1 Team debuts bio-based carbon fiber on W16 rear wheel shields developed using Syensqo resin with 30% bio-based materials.
The AAMMC aerospace tech hub earns $50 million in federal funding and private commitments to establish a Pacific Northwest test bed for aerospace composite materials, with partners including Syensqo.
ASCEND program completion marks the end of a 4-year, £39.6 million initiative charting progress in high-rate production, Industry 4.0, and sustainable composites manufacturing for auto and aerospace.
Plyable secures $3.2 million investment to advance custom composite marketplace solution, backed by Solvay Ventures and Maven Capital Partners.
Trillium awarded DOE funding for bio-based acrylonitrile demonstration plant, with project partners including Solvay, Koch Modular, and Matric.

CW Tech Days: Composites in advanced air mobility event is scheduled for Dec. 7th from 11 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. to discuss industry challenges and market demands.
JEC World 2026 Innovation Award finalists have been revealed, with 11 winners to be announced at the JEC Premiere on Jan. 12 in Paris.

facts: Mativ Holdings, Inc. is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company operates two segments: Filtration & Advanced Materials (FAM) and Sustainable & Adhesive Solutions (SAS).
Consolidated sales for the three months ended March 31, 2025 were $484.8 million, a decrease of 3.1% year over year and 0.2% on an organic basis.
GAAP loss for Q1 2025 was $425.5 million, including a non-cash goodwill impairment charge of $411.9 million and $14.5 million of organizational realignment and asset impairment expenses.
Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2025 was $37.2 million, down 19% versus the prior year period.
FAM segment sales were $187.6 million in Q1 2025, down 7.4% versus the prior year, reflecting lower volume/mix, lower selling prices, and unfavorable currency translation.
SAS segment sales were $297.2 million in Q1 2025, up 5.7% on an organic basis, driven by higher volume across all product categories excluding closed and divested plants.
FAM products include filtration media and components, advanced films, coating and converting solutions, and extruded mesh products, serving transportation and construction-related end markets.
SAS products include tapes, labels, liners, specialty paper, packaging, and healthcare solutions.
The company manufactures on three continents and generates sales in over 80 countries through its family of business-to-business and consumer product brands.
Mativ's broad portfolio combines polymers, fibers, and resins to optimize customer products across multiple stages of the value chain.

President and CEO Shruti Singhal stated the company is pivoting to a higher sense of urgency to drive enhanced commercial execution, de-lever the balance sheet, and conduct a strategic portfolio review.
Total debt was $1,122.8 million as of March 31, 2025, with cash and cash equivalents of $84.0 million, resulting in net debt of $1,038.8 million.
Total liquidity was approximately $407 million, consisting of $84 million of cash and cash equivalents and $323 million of revolver availability.
The company's debt matures on a staggered basis between 2027 and 2029.
Year-to-date 2025 cash used in operating activities was $15.9 million, with capital spending and software costs totaling $13.9 million.
Working capital was a $22.1 million use of cash due to an increase in accounts receivable, partially offset by a decrease in inventories and increase in accounts payable.
The company announced a quarterly cash dividend of $0.10 per share on May 7, 2025, payable on June 27, 2025.
During Q1 2025, the company did not repurchase any shares.
Risk factors highlight potential supply chain disruptions, including failures of energy, resin, fiber, and chemical suppliers to supply materials as needed.
The company faces risks from changes in employment, wage and hour laws, labor shortages, and labor strikes or stoppages.
Interest expense was $17.8 million for Q1 2025 versus $18.3 million in the prior year period.
Unallocated organizational realignment costs totaled $9.2 million in Q1 2025.

The FAM segment's adjusted EBITDA decreased 30% versus the prior year due to lower volume/mix, higher manufacturing and distribution costs, and unfavorable net selling price versus input cost performance.

The SAS segment's adjusted EBITDA increased $1.0 million (or more than 3%) compared to the prior year period, driven by higher volume across all product categories excluding closed/divested plants, and lower SG&A expenses.

canonical_name: Qualified Plug-in Electric Drive Motor Vehicle Credit (IRC 30D)
facts: The Qualified Plug-in Electric Drive Motor Vehicle Credit (IRC 30D), also referred to as the Clean Vehicle Credit, expired on September 30, 2025.
As of July 5, 2025, the credit is available for vehicles acquired on or before September 30, 2025.
A requirement for final assembly in North America took effect on August 17, 2022.
Additional requirements applied for vehicles placed in service (delivered) on or after January 1, 2023.
The amount of the credit depends on whether the vehicle meets critical minerals and battery components requirements for vehicles acquired after April 17, 2023.
Taxpayers who purchase an eligible vehicle may qualify for a tax credit of up to $7,500.
Beginning January 1, 2024, buyers can reduce the clean vehicle’s upfront purchase price by transferring their credit to the dealer.
Before 2024, eligible clean vehicle buyers could only receive the amount of their credit after filing their tax return.
Starting January 1, 2024, dealers must submit information to the IRS through IRS Energy Credits Online to determine vehicle eligibility and amount at the point of sale.
Without dealer submission, buyers cannot claim a tax credit on their return or transfer it to a dealer.
A dealer must provide the buyer a copy of the IRS’s approval of the dealer’s submission.
For vehicles delivered on or after April 18, 2023, limitations apply related to the vehicle’s MSRP, the buyer’s modified adjusted gross income, and the vehicle’s battery capacity.
A North American final assembly requirement applies for vehicles purchased on or after August 17, 2022.
Additional critical mineral and battery component requirements apply as of April 18, 2023, altering how the tax credit is calculated and potentially the amount available.
These latter requirements came into effect upon the publication of the Treasury Department’s guidance document regarding the critical mineral and battery component requirements.
Vehicles that meet the critical mineral requirements are eligible for a $3,750 tax credit.
Vehicles that meet the battery component requirements are eligible for a $3,750 tax credit.
Vehicles meeting both the critical mineral and the battery component requirements are eligible for a total tax credit of $7,500.
Vans, sport utility vehicles, and pickup trucks must not have an MSRP above $80,000.
All other vehicles may not have an MSRP above $55,000.
The MSRP includes any trim, options, or accessories for the particular vehicle and excludes the destination fee and dealer-provided options and accessories.
A taxpayer’s eligibility may be limited by thresholds for modified adjusted gross income (modified AGI) for the current tax year or the prior tax year.
The modified AGI threshold is $300,000 for joint filers.
The modified AGI threshold is $225,000 for head-of-household filers.
The modified AGI threshold is $150,000 for all other filers.
To be eligible for the Clean Vehicle Credit, the battery powering the vehicle must have a capacity of at least seven kilowatt-hours (kWh).

To be eligible for the $3,750 critical minerals portion, the percentage of the value of the battery’s critical minerals extracted or processed in the United States or a U.S. free-trade agreement partner, or recycled in North America, must meet specific thresholds.
The critical minerals minimum percent value requirement is 40% for 2023.
The critical minerals minimum percent value requirement is 50% for 2024.
The critical minerals minimum percent value requirement is 60% for 2025.
The critical minerals minimum percent value requirement is 70% for 2026.
The critical minerals minimum percent value requirement is 80% for 2027 and later.
To be eligible for the $3,750 battery components portion, the percentage of the value of the battery’s components manufactured or assembled in North America must meet specific thresholds.
The battery components minimum percent value requirement is 50% for 2023.
The battery components minimum percent value requirement is 60% for 2024 and 2025.
The battery components minimum percent value requirement is 70% for 2026.
The battery components minimum percent value requirement is 80% for 2027.
The battery components minimum percent value requirement is 90% for 2028.
The battery components minimum percent value requirement is 100% for 2029 and later.
Beginning January 1, 2023, the Clean Vehicle Credit provisions removed the manufacturer sales caps for vehicles sold after January 1, 2023.
The scope of eligible vehicles was expanded to include both EVs and FCEVs.

For vehicles placed in service before April 18, 2023, the available CVC tax credit is a base amount of $2,500 plus $417 for a vehicle that draws propulsion energy from a battery with at least 7 kWh of capacity.
An additional $417 is added for each kilowatt hour of battery capacity beyond 5 kWh.
The total tax credit available for a vehicle may not exceed $7,500.

Qualifying EVs purchased and delivered between August 17, 2022, and December 31, 2022, are eligible for the tax incentive as described for vehicles purchased before August 17, 2022, but are limited to vehicles with final assembly in North America.
Manufacturer sales caps on vehicles apply for purchases between August 17 and December 31, 2022.
For some manufacturers, the assembly location may vary because some models are produced in multiple locations.

The assembly location of a particular vehicle should be confirmed by referring to its Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) using the U.S. Department of Transportation’s VIN decoder or an information label affixed to the vehicle.
Qualifying EVs purchased before August 17, 2022, are eligible for a tax credit for a new qualified EV that draws propulsion from a battery with at least five kilowatt-hours (kWh) of capacity.
The qualifying EV must use an external source of energy to charge the battery.
The qualifying EV must have a gross vehicle weight rating of up to 14,000 pounds.
The qualifying EV must meet specified emission standards.
The minimum credit amount for pre-August 17, 2022 purchases is $2,500.
The credit may be up to $7,500 based on each vehicle’s traction battery capacity.

The credit will begin to be phased out for each manufacturer in the second quarter following the calendar quarter in which a minimum of 200,000 qualified PEVs have been sold by that manufacturer for use in the United States.

The tax credit is available for future EV owners with a written binding contract to purchase a new qualifying electric vehicle before August 16, 2022, who do not take possession until on or after August 16, 2022.
Jurisdiction for the program is Federal.
The program type is classified as Incentives.
The administering agency is the U.S. Internal Revenue Service.
The program was enacted on October 3, 2008.
The program was amended on July 4, 2025.
Covered technologies include EVs, Hydrogen Fuel Cells, and PHEVs.

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy's Advanced Materials and Manufacturing Technologies Office (AMMTO) funds manufacturing research and development projects through competitive solicitations known as funding opportunities, as well as prizes, lab calls, and requests for proposals from its network of manufacturing institutes.
AMMTO funds projects across three program areas: Next Generation Materials and Processes (NGMP), Energy Technology Manufacturing and Workforce (ETMW), and Secure and Sustainable Materials (SSM).

facts: Bulk sales of propane by licensed distributors are exempt from the Georgia state motor fuel excise tax.

Beginning Jan. 1, 2027, the sale of electricity dispensed from electric vehicle chargers owned by nonprofit organizations, located on federal interstates, and providing electricity without payment is exempt from the state motor fuel excise tax.

The Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) provides U.S. EPA Diesel Emission Reduction Act funding for eligible projects that replace existing school buses with alternative fuel or zero-emission school buses.
Eligible alternative fuels for the EPD diesel emission reduction grants include electricity, natural gas, and propane.

The U.S. DOT’s NEVI Formula Program required the Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) to submit an annually updated EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan for fiscal years 2022 through 2026 to their FHWA Division Office.
Georgia’s submitted NEVI plans must describe how the state intends to distribute NEVI funds, outline physical and cybersecurity strategies, and include a Community Engagement Outcomes Report.
The submitted NEVI plans must address updated NEVI Guidance.

A business that manufactures alternative energy products for use in battery, biofuel, and electric vehicle enterprises may claim an annual tax credit for five years under the Alternative Fuel and Advanced Vehicle Job Creation Tax Credit.
The amount of the job creation tax credit is based on the number of eligible new full-time employee jobs and the county where the business is located.
Qualified entities for the job creation tax credit must be defined as business enterprises and cannot include retail businesses.
Credit amounts for the job creation tax credit differ depending on how the county ranks based on unemployment rates and income levels.

An eligible business enterprise may claim an income tax credit for the purchase, lease, and installation of a qualified EV charging station located in Georgia under the EV Charging Station Tax Credit.
The EV charging station tax credit equals 10% of the cost of the station, up to $2,500.
Unused portions of the EV charging station tax credit may be carried forward for up to three years.
Any motor vehicle equipped with idle reduction technology may exceed Georgia state gross, axle, and tandem weight limits by up to 550 pounds.
To qualify for the idle reduction weight exemption, operators must present written certification of the technology's weight and demonstrate it is fully functional at all times.
Businesses can claim an income tax credit for 10% of the cost to convert a vehicle to natural gas, electricity, propane, or hydrogen, up to $2,500 per vehicle under the AFV Conversion Tax Credit.
Converted vehicles under the AFV Conversion Tax Credit must be fueled solely by an alternative fuel and meet low-emission or zero emission standards defined by the Board of Natural Resources.
Unused portions of the AFV conversion tax credit may be carried forward for up to three years.

The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) is a statewide initiative to grow the electric mobility industry through a partnership between government, industries, electric utilities, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
EMIA has a long-term goal to bring new investments to Georgia while supporting the ongoing transition of the state’s existing automotive industry.

The Georgia Department of Education provides grants to local educational agencies for the purchase of new electric, compressed natural gas, or propane school buses under the Alternative Fuel School Bus Grants program.
School districts applying for alternative fuel school bus grants must coordinate with a transportation field consultant from the Georgia Department of Education.
Alternative fuel school bus grant amounts vary and are available on a first-come, first-served basis.

Under Georgia law, a person or entity that provides EV charging services and is not subject to the jurisdiction of the Georgia Public Service Commission is not defined as a public utility.

A person can operate a fully autonomous vehicle with the automated driving system engaged without a driver present if the vehicle complies with federal motor vehicle safety standards and is registered as fully autonomous.
Gasoline suppliers providing fuel to distributors in Georgia must offer gasoline suitable for blending with fuel alcohol and cannot prevent distributors from qualifying for tax credits.
Effective January 1, 2027, all public EV charging stations in Georgia must accurately measure and display electricity dispensed to actively charging EVs on a per kilowatt-hour (kWh) basis.
The Georgia Department of Revenue has authority to conduct EV charging station inspections for accurate delivery and display of electricity per kWh, with violators subject to fines up to $1,000.
All-electric vehicles are subject to an annual licensing fee in 2025 of $352.56 for commercial vehicles and $234.97 for non-commercial vehicles.
Plug-in hybrid electric vehicles and flexible fuel vehicles are subject to the AFV annual licensing fee only if they have an AFV license plate.
AFV license plates include a one-time manufacturing fee of $25, an annual $20 registration fee, and a $35 special tag fee.
Electric, natural gas, propane, bi-fuel, and dual-fuel vehicles are eligible for an AFV license plate.
Biodiesel produced or sold in Georgia, including for blending with petroleum diesel, must meet ASTM Standard D6751.
Distributors selling or using motor fuel in Georgia are subject to an excise tax of $0.26 per gallon, adjustable based on vehicle efficiencies and the consumer price index.

Effective January 1, 2027, the gasoline gallon equivalent (GGE) for electricity may not exceed 11 kilowatt-hours, hydrogen must be at least 2.2 pounds, compressed natural gas must be at least 110,000 BTU, and liquefied natural gas must be at least 6.06 pounds.
Compressed natural gas (CNG) is defined as a mixture of hydrocarbon gases and vapors consisting principally of methane in gaseous form compressed for use as motor fuel.
Liquefied natural gas (LNG) is defined as methane or natural gas in cryogenic or refrigerated liquid form for use as motor fuel.
Propane and special fuels sold in bulk to a licensed consumer distributor are exempt from the alternative fuel excise tax.
The excise tax for electricity begins Jan.

facts: Since 2003, the DOE has awarded funding to support research, development, demonstration, and deployment of hydrogen and fuel cell technologies.

facts: The US automotive manufacturing industry is pivoting toward electric and hybrid vehicles driven by consumer demand and regulatory pressures, while increasing adoption of automation and robotics in manufacturing processes.
Chevrolet is actively involved in the transition to electric vehicles, showcasing models like the Equinox EV and Silverado EV.
Mazda Toyota Manufacturing is a joint venture between Mazda and Toyota located in Huntsville, Alabama, established in 2018.
Mazda Toyota Manufacturing specializes in assembling the Mazda CX-50 and Toyota Corolla Cross with an annual production capacity of 300,000 vehicles.
Mazda Toyota Manufacturing has made significant investments totaling $2.311 billion and employs around 1,466 people.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) is located in West Point, Georgia, serving as the first manufacturing site for Kia Corporation in North America.
KMMG began mass production on November 16, 2009, and has produced over 4.2 million vehicles.
The KMMG facility operates on a 2,200-acre site with an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
KMMG assembles the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, and K5 mid-size sedan.
KMMG recently expanded its production capabilities to include the all-electric EV9.
KMMG has created more than 14,000 jobs and emphasizes community engagement and workforce development.
Mercedes-Benz U.S. International, Inc. (MBUSI) is located in Vance, Alabama, established in 1993, and assembles the GLE and GLS SUVs.
MBUSI has seen significant investments exceeding $7 billion, including a recent $1 billion allocation for electric vehicle production and the establishment of a battery factory.
MBUSI has introduced training programs for aspiring automotive technicians to contribute to local workforce development.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, LLC (HMMA) is located in Montgomery, Alabama, founded in 2002 as Hyundai's first US assembly plant.
HMMA spans 3.4 million square feet and can produce up to 399,500 vehicles annually.
HMMA assembles the Tucson, Santa Fe, and Genesis GV70, and employs nearly 4,000 individuals.
HMMA operates three engine shops that produce engines for Hyundai and Kia vehicles.
BMW Manufacturing Co., LLC is located in Greer, South Carolina, with its Spartanburg facility being one of the largest BMW plants globally.
BMW Manufacturing employs over 10,000 individuals and produces hybrid models as part of a commitment to sustainability and electrified vehicles.
Dura Automotive Systems LLC specializes in electrification solutions and battery enclosures for battery electric vehicles.
Mitsubishi Motors is making strides in the electric vehicle market with models like the Outlander PHEV.
BMW of North America is actively involved in the transition towards electric mobility with a range of electrified vehicles.
source_title: Inven 2026

facts: Honda has provided a first look at its innovative approach to manufacture electric vehicles (EV) by late 2025 at the Honda EV Hub in Ohio.
The Honda EV Hub uniquely positions Honda to build internal combustion engine (ICE), hybrid-electric, and battery electric vehicles (EVs) on the same production lines.
An investment reaching over $1 billion is being used to retool the Marysville Auto Plant (MAP), East Liberty Auto Plant (ELP), and Anna Engine Plant (AEP).
The all-new Acura RSX SUV will be the first battery electric model produced at the Honda EV Hub in late 2025.
Production models based on the Honda 0 Saloon and Honda 0 SUV prototypes introduced at CES 2025 will follow the Acura RSX EV.
The retooled production facilities provide an enhanced work environment for associates and more sustainable production processes and equipment.

Bob Schwyn, senior vice president of Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC, stated that the Honda EV Hub provides flexibility to quickly respond to shifting customer needs and market conditions.
The Honda EV Hub will begin EV production in late 2025 with the all-new Acura RSX EV.
Honda debuted its original vehicle operating system (OS), the ASIMO OS, for use in Honda 0 Series models at CES 2025 in Las Vegas on January 7.
The use of the ASIMO name pays homage to the Honda ASIMO robot and the "Wise" value of the Honda 0 Series.
Honda announced plans to establish the Honda EV Hub in Ohio in October 2022, based on a vision to make zero emissions vehicles represent 100% of its vehicle sales by 2040.
Honda and LG Energy Solution committed to invest $3.5 billion in a new joint venture battery facility named L-H Battery Company.
The overall investment for the L-H Battery Company JV is projected to reach $4.4 billion.
The L-H Battery Company facility will have an annual capacity of approximately 40GWh, with production scheduled to start in late 2025.
The Honda EV Hub serves as the starting point for the global production of the new Honda 0 Series EVs.
The Honda EV Hub is developing knowledge and expertise for EV technology and production to be shared with other Honda plants in North America and globally.
Honda engineers implemented energy-saving and environmentally responsible initiatives aligned with the company's Triple Action to Zero approach, aimed at achieving zero environmental impact by 2050.
The Triple Action to Zero strategy focuses on three areas: carbon neutrality, clean energy, and resource circulation.
Honda is targeting net-zero CO2 emissions from its products and operations by 2050.
High efficiency melt furnaces installed at the Anna Engine Plant reduce natural gas consumption and can be modified in the future to Plasma Arc Melting to further reduce CO2 emissions.
New air HVAC handlers, more efficient lighting, and energy-saving equipment were introduced at all three plants to reduce the carbon footprint.

Honda's goal is to use 100% sustainable and/or carbon-free energy sources, already offsetting more than 60% of electricity used in North American manufacturing operations through long-term virtual power purchase agreements for renewable wind and solar power.
Honda aims to utilize 100% sustainable materials by reprocessing products back to raw materials and reusing them in new products.
Honda recycles scrap material at all plants, including aluminum at the Anna Engine Plant that is melted onsite and reused to make battery cases and engines.
Honda is transforming associate uniforms into sound-absorbing insulation for use in Honda and Acura automobiles.
Honda has produced automobiles in Ohio for over 40 years, beginning with automobile production at the Marysville Auto Plant in November 1982.
Honda established U.S. manufacturing operations in Marysville in 1979 with the start of motorcycle production.
Over 12,000 associates currently support Honda manufacturing in Ohio across five plants.

The five Ohio plants represent a total capital investment of $13 billion and have the capacity to produce 460,000 Honda and Acura automobiles, 1.18 million automobile engines, and over 1 million automobile transmissions and two-motor hybrid systems.

The company's five Ohio manufacturing facilities include the Marysville Auto Plant, East Liberty Auto Plant, Performance Manufacturing Center, Anna Engine Plant, and a transmission plant in Russells Point.
Honda has invested more than $1.4 billion in these Ohio facilities over the past six years to modernize equipment and adopt new technologies.
The Marysville Auto Plant consolidated its two production lines to create capability for producing EVs on the same line with ICE and hybrid vehicles.
MAP is the first Honda plant globally to assemble EV battery packs.
Retooling at MAP involved installing new weld robots and technology to enable welding EV bodies on the same line with ICE models, including changes to secure the EV battery case in the vehicle floor.
MAP is adding CDC welding for the first time at Honda globally to expand the use of lightweight and high-strength materials.
Engineers renovated an area at MAP to create a workspace for assembling battery packs that combine modules from L-H Battery Company with battery case megacasts from the Anna Engine Plant.
The EV battery pack assembly line at MAP will be the longest automated guided vehicle (AGV) line at Honda.
A new dedicated area on the MAP production line was established for installing battery packs into EVs, allowing ICE and hybrid vehicles to pass through without stopping.
A new hybrid-electric battery assembly area was established closer to the installation area and expanded to meet record customer demand for hybrid-electric models.

The Vehicle Quality Department at MAP evolved with new optical aiming station improvements to check alignment and functioning of safety and connectivity technologies in EVs and future software-defined vehicles.
The updated VQD accommodates radar and optical aiming verification required for L3 automated driving features on Honda 0 Series vehicles.
MAP associates have begun new training programs to prepare for skills required for EV production, EV battery pack assembly, and quality checks for software-defined vehicles.
The Anna Engine Plant is adding megacasting of EV battery cases, which will serve as the main frame structure for the floor of Honda and Acura EVs.
AEP installed six 6,000-ton high-pressure die cast machines to megacast EV battery case parts in a mass production first for global Honda.
The battery cases are made in two halves welded together later, enabling production of two different sizes for midsize and large EVs with one-half common to each size.
Installation of the die-cast machines required modifications including placement of pilons 80 feet below the concrete surface to support the 31-feet tall machines.
AEP uses high-efficiency natural gas melt furnaces that reduced compressed air usage compared to current furnaces and can be modified to Plasma Arc Melting in the future.
AEP recycles 100% of scrap aluminum from the die-cast process to be reused in-house for case and engine production.
AEP is using Friction Stir Welding (FSW) as a mass production first globally to join the two halves of the aluminum battery case and attach an aluminum water jacket stamped at MAP.
The aluminum water jacket maintains optimal battery module temperature to maximize battery efficiency.
AEP will employ Photogrammetry using four machines with 90 cameras to visually analyze part shape post-die-cast, and two machines with 120 cameras prior to shipping to ensure mountability.
The photogrammetry quality checks adopt a human-friendly approach that does not require associates to lift or manually check heavy battery cases.
AEP adopted Minimum Quantity Lubrication (MQL) machining for the aluminum battery case, reducing energy use and lubricant requirements.
ELP built a new paint department and strengthened carriers for heavier EV bodies, including a phosphate and E-coat expansion nearing completion to achieve higher paint quality.
ELP constructed a new Vehicle Quality Department building north of the plant with a new optical aiming station to check alignment and functioning of safety and connectivity technologies.

Assembly department infrastructure at ELP, including drop lifts, conveyors, and support beams, is being strengthened to handle heavier EV models and offer flexibility for hybrid-electric and ICE models.
A new approach with AGVs on the ELP final assembly line provides a stronger, larger body carrier for EVs.
The Battery Pack Mount Mezzanine at ELP enables EV models to disengage from the main assembly line for battery pack installation before rejoining the existing line.
Weld changepoints at ELP include reinforcement and modifications to existing drop lifts, transfers, and conveyor systems to accommodate EVs.
source_title: Honda EV Hub Prepares for New Level of Flexible Production in Reimagined Manufacturing Environment
