facts: Archer Aviation has an exclusive manufacturing partnership with Stellantis through 2030, which supplies up to $150 million in equity and automotive-grade manufacturing know-how.
The Stellantis partnership allows Archer to avoid approximately $1.2-1.5 billion in capex for a high-volume production line.
Archer is scaling its Covington, Georgia plant toward 650 aircraft per year by 2026 under the exclusive contract with Stellantis.
By 2026, Archer shifted to high-rate manufacturing at its Covington, GA facility, targeting 1,000 Midnight aircraft per year.
Collaboration with Stellantis cut assembly time by 30% and aims to lower the cost per unit to approximately $3.2 million from prototype costs of ~$6.5 million in FY2025.
Archer is accelerating fulfillment of a backlog of 2,300 orders from global airline partners at its Georgia facility.

The 350,000 square foot high-volume manufacturing facility in Georgia is co-backed by Stellantis and planned to scale to annual production in the low thousands, with a company target of approximately 3,000 aircraft per year by 2028.
The Georgia facility supports a reported capital investment of $200-250 million in 2025.

Building Covington and robotic lines demanded roughly $250m-$300m in 2025 capex, with Stellantis covering about $100m while Archer records ~$150m-$200m for aerospace tooling and battery assembly equipment.

Battery technology R&D and thermal management system optimization are critical to Archer's profitability, with approximately $120 million spent on battery and thermal R&D in FY2025 to extend cell cycle life and lower lifetime cost per flight.

In 2026, Archer aims for a 10-minute turnaround charge between urban hops, targeting over 3,000 cycle equivalent life to halve replacement frequency and reduce per-flight battery cost by approximately 40%.
Battery pack replacement costs are estimated at $250k-$400k per aircraft after 1,500-3,000 cycles, driving maintenance and depreciation per flight hour.
Lithium and nickel price swings can change the battery cost per flight hour by an estimated 10-25%.
Archer must secure high-performance cell supply and invest in recycling, with capex for recycling scaling in the tens of millions of dollars.

Archer runs simulator-based training academies to convert commercial pilots to eVTOL operations, reporting a training capacity of approximately 120 pilots per year in 2025 to meet safety and regulatory standards.
Human capital at Archer includes many hires from Tesla, Apple, and Boeing, combining automotive mass-production know-how with aerospace safety rigor.
Archer reported approximately 260 engineering staff in 2025 driving its VX program and achieving FAA Part 23 milestones in 2025.
United Airlines provided a $1.0 billion conditional purchase agreement and $10 million pre-delivery payment, remaining Archer's commercial cornerstone into 2026.
United Airlines' 2025 guidance showed it expects 50-70 daily premium feeder flights per hub, signaling a demand pipeline that de-risks Archer's Midnight ramp.
Southwest Airlines signed an MOU for California network operations targeting intra-city travel and seeking to link eVTOL flights into Southwest's approximately 2,200 weekly California departures.
Atlantic Aviation and REEF Technology have vertiport infrastructure agreements with Archer, giving access to over 100 airports via Atlantic and over 6,000 urban parking/merchant sites via REEF.

The Abu Dhabi Investment Office and Mubadala committed a multi-hundred million dollar package (reported $300-400m by 2025) to build Abu Dhabi's air taxi ecosystem, giving Archer a regulatory fast-track and revenue runway as the UAE launch market by early 2026.

Archer maintains a specialized relationship with military stakeholders via AFWERX and the US Air Force, targeting logistics and medical evacuation use cases that yielded $15.6 million in non-dilutive DoD awards in FY2025.
The dual-use positioning of civil air taxi plus defense MEDEVAC supports revenue diversification into government contracts worth an estimated $40 million pipeline in 2025.

Archer provides InterGlobe and UAE operators multi-year SLAs covering technical support and fleet maintenance, targeting over 99% aircraft availability and reducing unscheduled downtime by approximately 30% versus ad-hoc support.
2025 service contracts total approximately $120 million ARR, feeding a predictable feedback loop for iterative product updates.

Archer's proprietary app manages bookings, weight-and-balance, and vertiport slots, supporting single-ticket journeys via API links to airline partners; in FY2025 the platform processed approximately 120,000 bookings.

The fleet orchestration engine routes aircraft and schedules charging to boost utilization, with Archer reporting a target of 6-8 daily cycles per aircraft and projecting $1.2 million annual revenue per aircraft in FY2025 optimized operations.

As a commercial operator under FAA Part 135, Archer manages a 12-aircraft fleet, schedules approximately 180 pilot shifts monthly, and must meet commercial safety benchmarks that drove a $42.7 million 2025 operating compliance spend to maintain dispatch reliability above 99.2%.
With FAA Part 145 certification, Archer conducts in-house maintenance, cutting average aircraft downtime to 24 hours and saving an estimated $6.3 million in 2025 outsourced MRO costs.

Every Archer aircraft sale includes a long-term MRO service agreement for parts, certified maintenance, and battery swaps, creating a razor-and-blade model where a $1.2 million average unit sale (2025 target ASP) drives decades of high-margin recurring revenue.
Archer projects $150k lifetime MRO revenue per aircraft and $45M ARR by 2025 from services.

Archer licenses ArcherOS to third-party operators for flight planning, charging schedules, and passenger manifests, generating high-margin SaaS fees; in FY2025 software revenue contributed an estimated $45 million, with gross margins above 80%.

Archer secures milestone and R&D grant payments via AFWERX, receiving cost-plus contract reimbursements that de-risk military-grade feature development; FY2025 awards and milestones contributed roughly $12.4 million in revenue and $3.1 million in R&D reimbursement through defense programs.
Archer maintains strategic liquidity with over $400 million in cash and equivalents as of FY2025, funded by equity raises, strategic investments, and customer pre-delivery payments.
Pre-delivery deposits total approximately $85 million.
Planned burn coverage is approximately 18-24 months at current run-rate.
Archer's IP covers the Midnight configuration-tilt-rotor geometry tuned for hover efficiency and 200+ kt cruise; patents protect blade, nacelle and control laws.
The 12-tilt-motor redundancy lets Midnight safely land after multiple failures, with a certification target of FAR 23-equivalent, 12/12 fault-tolerant cases.
Archer targets $3.00-$6.00 per passenger-mile to position itself as premium mass transit, aiming for Uber Black-competitive airport transfers by 2026 to drive load factors and rapid adoption.
Archer's Midnight is approximately 100x quieter than traditional helicopters, producing approximately 55 dB at cruise versus 85-100 dB for helicopters, helping secure municipal vertiport approvals.
Archer projects this noise reduction enables 2-3x higher flight frequency in noise-sensitive zones and supports a 2025 target of 1,000 daily flights in initial markets.
Archer cuts 60-90 minute drives to 10-20 minute flights, saving 50-70 minutes per trip; at $79 average fare pilot pricing and projected 2025 MBi per seat of $3,500-5,000 annually.

Archer Aviation offers zero tailpipe emissions versus helicopters and ICE vehicles, cutting CO2 per passenger-mile and supporting corporate ESG goals; in 2025 Archer targets commercial ops with a projected fleet reducing urban transport emissions by up to 70% vs cars.
By 2026, Archer is fully integrated into United Airlines MileagePlus, allowing passengers to earn/redeem miles on Archer's eVTOL hops, driving repeat use among United's 125M+ global members.
Pilots report 35% higher repeat bookings on MileagePlus-linked routes, turning short flights into door-to-hub travel chains.
Archer Air keeps non-airline customers in a direct relationship via its branded app, handling ID verification, baggage-weight checks, and payments to deliver a premium, high-touch booking flow.

The app is the main data source for demand-led scheduling; in 2025 Archer Air reported 120,000 app bookings (64% of retail trips) and used that data to boost load factor by 6 percentage points versus legacy routing.

Archer's high-touch concierge at branded vertiport lounges creates a physical premium experience like an airline club; Archer reported 2025 vertiport lounge operating pilots handling 12,000 customers and contributing to targeted yield of $85 per passenger vs. $20-30 for premium ground transit.
Integrated booking via United.com and the United Airlines app drives interline volume, with 36% of Archer Aviation's 2025 bookings (approximately 18,000 rides) coming as first-leg transfers.
Physical vertiports and FBO terminals double as stations and billboards; Manhattan's Downtown Heliport offered approximately 1.2M annual heli-passenger sightings pre-2025.
Archer targets 50+ vertiports by 2028, with initial 2025 pilots focused on high-visibility urban sites.
Corporate travel management platforms and GDS integration (Concur and Navan) allow corporate travel teams to add eVTOL transfers to itineraries, ensuring policy compliance and expense reporting.
Archer targets the $1.3T global business travel market (2025) and aims to capture high-yield city-hopper segments worth approximately $15B TAM for eVTOL corporate transfers by 2030.

In markets like India and the Middle East, Archer Aviation uses regional distributors and operating partners to run sales and ground ops, leveraging local regulatory know-how and infrastructure so Archer avoids large local footprints.
Partners handled approximately 75% of regional market entry tasks in 2025, cutting capex by an estimated $18-22M per market.
Time-sensitive airport commuters and business travelers are Archer's primary segment; surveys show 45 minutes saved is worth a $100 premium to 62% of NYC-area professionals.
Peak Tuesday-Thursday business windows capture approximately 68% of trips between Manhattan and Newark, supporting $120-150 average fare yields in 2025.

High-net-worth individuals and super-commuters value Archer Aviation's eVTOL for saving time; the Midnight aircraft targets 60+ mph equivalent cruise and approximately 100-150 mile urban ranges, cutting typical 90-120 minute drives.
Premium buyers with household net worth over $1.5M pay for privacy, speed, and prestige tied to Archer's brand and expected 2025 city air mobility launch timelines.

Archer's eVTOL can capture a high-margin niche transporting time-critical medical supplies, blood, and organs; market estimates show urgent medical logistics services grew 8% in 2025 to $5.6B globally, with organ transports valuing approximately $420M.

The US Department of Defense and federal agencies prize Archer for quiet, attritable logistics and personnel movement in contested zones, valuing its low acoustic signature and runway-independent VTOL capability.
US DoD interest targets multi-year buys and fleet service contracts often exceeding $100M per program.
Recent procurement studies cite attritable aircraft ops lowering mission cost-per-flight by 30% versus manned helicopters and DoD R&D awards to eVTOL firms totaled $85M in 2025.
Early tech adopters and premium experience seekers will drive initial 2026 demand, with Archer expecting these flyers to account for approximately 40-50% of launch bookings.
Premium fares for early adopters range from $1,200-$2,500 per flight, yielding high initial margins and $10-20M in first-year revenue uplift while generating viral social media reach.

Archer allocates roughly $140-170 million (approximately 12-15% of 2025 pro forma burn of $1.15B) to R&D for next-gen aircraft and autonomy, funding software engineering, sensor-fusion R&D, and advanced materials testing to enable fully autonomous flight and remove pilot cost.

As a Part 135 operator in FY2025, Archer's largest recurring opex is labor: pilots (avg base pay approximately $120k-$160k/year) and certified A&P technicians (approximately $70k-$95k/year) at each vertiport, driving approximately 40-55% of operating costs.
Archer targets automation to cut this labor burden by 20-30% over 5 years.

Operating in urban airspace drives high insurance premiums; Archer Aviation reported projected insurance and certification costs contributing to per-flight costs of about $25-$40 in early commercial operations (2025 estimates).
Vertiport slot fees and landing charges act as fixed per-arrival/departure tolls; market reports estimate $5-$15 per movement depending on city and operator agreements.

Archer Air's primary B2C revenue is per-seat ticket sales, capturing full fare directly from riders with dynamic pricing tied to demand, time of day, and weather; average ticket estimates for 2025 range $75-$150 per trip in launch cities.
Archer generates lumpy revenue from Midnight aircraft sales at typically about $5.0M per unit, with large pre-delivery payments that fund operations.
The backlog tied to the $1.0B United Airlines order remains a multi-year revenue stream supporting liquidity.
source_title: Archer: Business Model Canvas – businessmodelcanvastemplate.com

canonical_name: U.S. Electricity Market and Grid Infrastructure Trends
facts: U.S. electricity consumption reached record levels in 2022 at approximately 4,067 billion kWh.
National electricity demand is projected to climb to 4,165 billion kWh in 2025, reflecting roughly a 13% increase from 2022 to 2025.
The electrification of transportation via more electric vehicles (EVs) on the road and a shift to electric heat pumps is accelerating grid load.
The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) notes that homes and businesses are using more electricity for transportation and heating, contributing to demand growth.
Data centers could consume around 9% of total U.S. electricity by 2030, driven by hyperscalers and AI workloads.
The Biden Administration announced $1.3 billion in late 2023 to expand transmission lines nationwide, adding an estimated 3.5 GW of transfer capacity.
In 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy committed $1.5 billion for new transmission projects under its Transmission Facilitation Program.
Goldman Sachs projects that 85–90 gigawatts of new nuclear generation capacity might be required globally to meet data center-driven demand.
Two new reactors at Plant Vogtle in Georgia came online in 2023 and 2024, marking the first large-scale U.S. nuclear units in 30 years.
The Department of Energy is actively supporting advanced reactor technologies, including small modular reactors, and securing high-assay low-enriched uranium fuel for next-generation plants.
Retail electricity prices in the U.S. have increased faster than general inflation since 2022, with a projected ~13% jump in the national average from 2022 to 2025.
EV charging networks and electrified heating are contributing to new heights in grid peak demand periods.
Utilities are deploying thicker-gauge wires, higher-capacity transformers, and advanced grid control systems in high-growth corridors to handle increased loads.
Public utility commissions are permitting rate increases specifically to fund critical transmission investments needed for grid modernization.
Some utilities are exploring vehicle-to-grid (V2G) integration, where EVs or customer-sited batteries could supply power back to the grid as distributed support resources.

Tech companies including Google, Microsoft, and Meta have announced 100% renewable energy goals and are buying vast quantities of wind and solar power through long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) spanning 10–20 years.
Utilities are adding utility-scale batteries to store excess solar or wind energy and release it during peak demand periods.
For critical new loads like hyperscale data centers, utilities often build N+1 redundancy into serving substations and feeders to ensure uninterrupted power supply.
source_title: Surging U.S. Electricity Demand Amid an AI Data Center Boom

facts: Nippon Denkai Ltd., headquartered in Tokyo, Japan, is planning to dissolve and liquidate its American subsidiary, Denkai America Inc. (DAI), located in Camden, South Carolina.
The dissolution was revealed in a letter announcing Nippon Denkai’s petition for “civil rehabilitation proceedings,” which are analogous to Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the U.S.
The rehabilitation petition was filed in Tokyo District Court on Nov. 27.
The company canceled an “extraordinary shareholders” meeting that had been scheduled for Nov. 29.
DAI manufactures electrolytic copper foils that are added by other manufacturers of microchips for use in electric vehicle (EV) batteries.
The company invested ¥30 billion (~$225 million) to build the Camden plant in 2018.
DAI received $4.6 million in state tax credits from the South Carolina Economic Development Corp in 2019.
Total investment for the facility is approximately ¥30 billion ($225 million).
The company canceled debt of ¥10 billion (~$75 million) with SPX, a major customer.
For the financial year ended March 31, 2026, DAI reported revenue of ¥1.5 billion (~$11 million) and a loss of ¥0.8 billion (~$6 million).
The company is projecting a $1.7 billion (~$1.2 billion) investment for a new facility and a workforce of 2,000 employees.
Camden County Economic Development Corp CEO Jonathan Slesson stated the county is “looking for a buyer” for the facility.
Nippon Denkai CEO Richard Goddard said the company has been “losing money” since 2019.
South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster offered incentives to support the project.
John B. Smith, Executive Director of the SC EDC, was involved in incentive discussions for the facility.
SPX will continue to supply electrolytic copper foils to Nippon Denkai’s former customers.
A new plant location is planned for Camden County, near Camden.
Total debt as of March 31, 2026, was reported at approximately ¥1.5 billion (~$11 million).
source_title: Denkai files for bankruptcy, plans to liquidate Camden plant | Camden | pmg-sc.com

facts: Novelis Inc. is constructing a state-of-the-art greenfield rolling and recycling plant in Bay Minette, Alabama.
The company describes the Bay Minette facility as a significant U.S. investment intended to bring needed capacity to an undersupplied domestic market.

Total capital expenditures reached $913 million for the first six months of fiscal year 2026, primarily driven by strategic investments in new rolling and recycling capacity under construction, most notably the Bay Minette plant.
Start-up costs related to the construction of the Bay Minette rolling and recycling plant are included in Selling, general and administrative expenses.
Demand for infinitely recyclable, lightweight aluminum continues to grow as a fundamental material in modern transportation, according to CEO Steve Fisher.
Automotive shipments saw slightly higher volumes in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026, contributing to total rolled product shipments of 941 kilotonnes.
Novelis supplies aluminum products and solutions to customers in the automotive, aerospace, beverage packaging, and specialties industries across North America, Europe, Asia, and South America.
Novelis Inc. is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group.
source_title: Novelis Reports Second Quarter Fiscal Year 2026 Results

facts: Murata Electronics North America, Inc. manufactures and distributes a vast array of electronic components.
The company operates manufacturing facilities in Rockmart, Georgia and State College, Pennsylvania.
Murata is one of the world's largest manufacturers of fixed and variable ceramic capacitors.

Product lines include potentiometers, piezo alarms, resistor networks, posistors, piezoelectric ceramic filters and resonators, crystal filters and oscillators, EMI/RFI filters, high voltage components, and hybrid circuits.
Distribution centers in Georgia and the Northeast support authorized distributors to assure fast delivery.
An experienced staff of application engineers is available at all facilities to provide technical support for Murata products.
Component reliability is verified through approvals to UL, CSA, and Military specifications.
The GRM series features monolithic ceramic chip capacitors in industry standard sizes including 0603, 0805, 1206, 1210, 1812, 1825, 2220, and 2225.
Capacitor temperature characteristics include COG (NP0), X7R (+15%), Z5U (+22%, -56%), and Y5V (+22%, -82%).
Automatic insertion packaging is available in 8mm and 12mm tape & reel formats.
Standard termination materials feature a nickel barrier layer, with palladium silver and tin plating available upon request.
The RPE series provides conformal coated radial lead capacitors with voltage ratings ranging from 50V to 500VDC.
Temperature compensating variants (N150, N220, N330, N470, N750) are available for the RPE series.

entity_name: Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech
canonical_name: Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech
facts: Novelis and Georgia Tech have partnered since 2019 through the Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech to advance research and business opportunities in aluminum manufacturing.
The Novelis Global Research and Technology Center (NGRTC) is located in Kennesaw, GA.
On September 24, 2025, the 2025 Novelis Scholars Day was held from 8 am to 3 pm at the NGRTC in Kennesaw, GA.

At the September 24, 2025 event, Novelis Graduate and Undergraduate Scholars presented research projects on topics including converting CO2 to multi-carbon chemicals, redox mechanisms in lithium-ion batteries, sustainable energy utilization, predictive modeling for streaming data, defect control in bimetallic wire-arc DED structures, quality control in composite manufacturing, causal models for cascading failures in distributed manufacturing networks, low-temperature lithium-ion batteries, and fatigue prediction of 316 stainless steel.
The September 24, 2025 Scholars Day was hosted by Dr. Sazol Das (R&D Leader) and Dr. Vinicius Silva (Senior Director, Regional R&D) at Novelis.
Georgia Tech leadership at the September 24, 2025 event was represented by Professor Shreyes Melkote, Executive Director of the Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech.
Opening remarks for the September 24, 2025 event were delivered by Dr. Silva, Professor Melkote, and Dr. Das.

Novelis scientists and engineers attended the September 24, 2025 event in person and virtually, followed by a networking lunch and guided tours of Novelis research laboratories and pilot-scale production facilities.
On March 27, 2025, the Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech organized its annual Novelis Industry Day event at Georgia Tech as a hybrid gathering.
The March 27, 2025 Industry Day was co-hosted by Dr. Shreyes Melkote and Ms. Tina Kosmicki (Head of Process Innovation for Manufacturing Excellence at Novelis).
Over 45 Novelis scientists and engineers, along with Georgia Tech faculty and students, participated in the March 27, 2025 Industry Day.

Dr. Melkote welcomed participants on behalf of Dr. Julia Kubanek (Vice President of Interdisciplinary Research at Georgia Tech) and highlighted 2024 Hub achievements while introducing the 2025 Novelis Scholars.
Mr. John Bachner (Sr. R&D Director for Americas at Novelis) presented on Novelis' 3X30 sustainability goals during the March 27, 2025 event.

The March 27, 2025 Industry Day format included presentations by Novelis technical leads on technology development needs and by Georgia Tech faculty on sponsored research projects in energy management, next-generation renewable energy devices, and heat exchanger design.
Georgia Tech researchers presented new capabilities in roll-to-roll manufacturing at the AMPF, as well as hot rolling and robotic forging.
On October 29, 2024, the third Novelis Scholars Day was held from 8 am to 3 pm at the NGRTC in Kennesaw, GA.

At the October 29, 2024 event, scholars presented research on grid technologies, augmented reality in manufacturing, corrosion behavior of AL 7050 alloys, wire arc directed energy deposition, computational alloy design, amine-based sorbents for direct air capture, Bayesian materials design, polar optoelectronics, low-temperature batteries, perovskite crystallinity, and solid oxide cell electrode materials.
The October 29, 2024 Scholars Day was hosted by Ms. Tina Kosmicki (Senior Manager of R&D Ecosystems) and Dr. Raj Gopalaswamy (Global Technology Director of New Domains at Novelis).

Mr. John Bachner joined the October 29, 2024 event for student presentations, followed by a networking lunch and tours of Novelis research laboratories and a beverage can pilot manufacturing facility.
Novelis and Georgia Tech co-hosted the 19th International Conference on Aluminum Alloys (ICAA19) at Georgia Tech's campus for four days.

ICAA19 attracted over 300 participants from 19 countries, featuring 12 symposia topics and over 250 technical presentations focused on sustainability, future mobility, and next-generation manufacturing.

Keynote addresses at ICAA19 were delivered by leaders from the Aluminum Association, Airbus, and Coca-Cola, with Novelis CTO Philippe Meyer and Georgia Tech EVP for Research Chaouki Abdallah headlining the event.
The ICAA19 conference marked the fifth anniversary of the Novelis Innovation Hub at Georgia Tech, which has been active since 2019.
On February 29, 2024, the Novelis Innovation Hub successfully conducted its Industry Day event in-person and virtually at the Georgia Tech Manufacturing Institute (GTMI).
The February 29, 2024 Industry Day focused on reviewing progress and outcomes of basic and applied research projects funded by Novelis through the Innovation Hub in 2023.
Opening remarks for the February 29, 2024 event were delivered by Dr. Julia Kubanek (GT) and Dr. Philippe Meyer (Novelis CTO), followed by a sustainability goals overview by Dr. Raj Gopalaswamy.

Georgia Tech faculty at the February 29, 2024 event presented research on advanced battery materials, heat exchanger design, sustainable manufacturing, aluminum in renewable energy, high throughput testing, and molten metal flow behavior in casting.
Ms. Tina Kosmicki highlighted internship and co-op opportunities at Novelis for Georgia Tech students during the February 29, 2024 event.

Discussions at the February 29, 2024 event covered joint technical workshops, AMPF research infrastructure, and plans to organize ICAA19 in 2024 and the International Deep Drawing Research Group (IDDRG) conference in 2028.
On September 28, 2023, the second Novelis Scholars Day was held from 8 am to 3 pm at the NGRTC in Kennesaw, GA, featuring seven graduate and four undergraduate scholars.

Scholar presentations at the September 28, 2023 event covered energy justice, continuum mechanics, perovskites for solar cells, thermal desalination, catalytic interfaces, wire arc additive manufacturing geometry control, pro-environmental behaviors, Ti-glycerol hybrids for fuel cells, and the urban heat island effect.

The September 28, 2023 event was hosted by Ms. Tina Kosmicki and Dr. Raj Gopalaswamy, with attendees including Dr. Jim Fekete (Director of R&D for North Americas) and Georgia Tech faculty Professors Shreyes Melkote and Juan-Pablo Correa-Baena.
In October 2023, Novelis donated a Bruker S2 Ranger XRF system to Georgia Tech's Materials Innovation and Learning Laboratory (MILL) for undergraduate and graduate research projects and lab courses.

A Georgia Tech research team led by Professor Matthew McDowell is using aluminum foil to create batteries with higher energy density and greater stability, a project that began as a collaboration between GT and Novelis through the Innovation Hub and was detailed in Nature Communications.

The Novelis Scholars program selected seven graduate and four undergraduate scholars for the 2022-2023 academic year, following an inaugural group of six graduate students selected from 34 nominations for the 2021-2022 academic year.
ICAA19 was scheduled for June 23 to 27, 2024, in Atlanta, with a call for abstracts in June 2023, an abstract deadline in November 2023, and a final program in December 2023.

On June 8, 2023, the Novelis Innovation Hub organized an AI Applications Workshop at Georgia Tech to identify synergies in AI/ML for manufacturing systems modeling, materials informatics, digital twins, and model-based system engineering.
The June 8, 2023 workshop brought together over 50 Novelis scientists and engineers with GT faculty and scholars to develop collaborative proposals and statements of work.

On February 2, 2023, a hybrid Industry Day at Georgia Tech reviewed 2022 funded research projects covering advanced battery materials, sustainable engineering modeling, energy management analytics, new alloy development, high throughput testing, casting fundamentals, and aluminum surface functionalization.

On September 27, 2022, the first Novelis Scholars Day was held from 9 am to 3 pm at the NGRTC in Kennesaw, GA, where six inaugural graduate scholars presented on ammonia production, perovskite deposition, NiTi shape memory alloys, AM 7050 alloys, fault diagnosis, and microstructure analysis.

On March 31, 2022, Novelis representatives Dr. Raj Gopalaswamy, Dr. Sarmistha Das, Suzanne Haupt, and Cullen Pearson participated in the WiMSE Connect and Dine event at the Academy of Medicine in midtown Atlanta.

On August 30, 2021, a leadership meeting was held at Georgia Tech featuring Novelis CEO Steve Fisher and SVP/CTO Philippe Meyer alongside GT President Cabrera, EVP Chaouki Abdallah, and VP Julia Kubanek.
source_title: Novelis News & Events | Research

facts: Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, GA.
The company powers applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
Stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Stryten Energy supports a technology-agnostic approach to large-scale energy storage, with lead, lithium, and vanadium playing significant roles in the future of energy storage.

At the Vicars Community Center in Atlanta, GA, a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) connected to a solar installation powers the Vicars Community Resilience Hub to provide reliable power during outages and severe weather.

Stryten Energy is partnering with Snapping Shoals EMC in Covington, GA, to demonstrate advanced vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) technology for energy storage and evaluate its maximum benefits for energy providers.

Scott Childers, Vice President of Stryten Energy’s Essential Power Division, stated that the collaboration with Snapping Shoals will provide a deeper understanding of how VRFB technology operates in real-world conditions.

On April 23, 2025, Georgia Tech and Stryten Energy unveiled the installation of a lead battery energy storage system at Georgia Tech to help achieve carbon-neutral goals alongside existing solar installations.

On March 31, 2025, Stryten Energy announced a plan to add 10 gigawatts of new U.S. energy storage manufacturing capacity to increase domestic supply for military, long-duration energy storage, transportation, and material-handling applications.

Stryten Energy launched Storion Energy, a joint venture with Largo based in Alpharetta, GA, on December 19, 2024, to establish a domestic supply of vanadium electrolyte for long-duration energy storage flow batteries.
The Storion Energy joint venture will deliver price-competitive vanadium electrolyte via a unique leasing model to drive rapid commercialization and adoption of vanadium redox flow batteries.

Stryten Energy was awarded DOE MAKE IT Prize Phase 2 funding on October 2, 2024, for the Securing America’s Vanadium Electrolyte Supply (SAVES) project to scale U.S.-based production and commercialization of cost-effective vanadium redox flow battery electrolyte.
The Inflation Reduction Act provides incentives like the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit (AMPC) for companies investing in domestic clean energy manufacturing.

On August 28, 2024, Stryten Energy opened an expanded, state-of-the-art battery testing lab at its new corporate headquarters in Alpharetta, GA, dedicated to testing lead, lithium, and vanadium batteries and materials.

Stryten Energy signed a licensing agreement with Dragonfly Energy to exclusively market and distribute the Battle Born Batteries® brand of lithium-ion batteries for military, automotive, marine, power sports, lawn & garden, and golf cart applications.
On April 13, 2026, Stryten Energy launched the U.S.-manufactured M-Series Li610 lithium-ion battery, designed to keep material handling operations moving with longer battery cycle life.
On April 14, 2026, Stryten Energy announced expanded capabilities of the M-Series X-7 charger at MODEX 2026, highlighting updates that expand compatibility, power, and flexibility.

Stryten Energy's new E-Series AGM Multi-Terminal Batteries (AGM160/190) are designed for harsh environments, offering superior performance in long-duration discharge applications with a spill-proof design.
Enhanced Flooded Batteries (EFB) offer a lower-cost alternative to AGM batteries for start-stop and hybrid vehicles, providing about 50% higher capacity and up to 52%...
The U.S. Department of Energy includes lead batteries among critical minerals, underscoring the vital role of recycled lead in domestic battery manufacturing and national security.
North America’s closed-loop lead-battery recycling system achieves high efficiency in the circular economy.
Power demand in the United States is projected to reach 4,185 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025.
Stryten Energy provides premium battery and charger solutions through KION North America’s network of 200 sales and service locations for nationwide access.
On October 15, 2024, Stryten Energy invested in REVKIT to become the preferred provider of advanced automotive batteries on their digital platform for the automotive enthusiast community.
Jeremy Furr, Senior Vice President of Strategic Sourcing at Stryten Energy, emphasizes the importance of a domestic supply chain amid geopolitical tensions and critical mineral bans.
China’s export ban on critical minerals including antimony, gallium, and germanium, effective December 3, 2024, is disrupting battery manufacturing supply chains.
Stryten Energy showcased lead battery energy storage system technology at Battcon 2024 for microgrids and EV-charging applications.
The company's inCOMMAND™ IoT platform enables efficient fleet management with enhanced customization, faster performance, and improved mobile usability.
Stryten Energy's mobile microgrid solution is designed for remote power and disaster relief, demonstrating a resilient hybrid diesel-electric energy ecosystem.
At CES 2026, Stryten Energy highlighted technology-agnostic BESS solutions supporting data centers, manufacturing, and EV charging infrastructure.
The company focuses on domestically sourced and manufactured energy storage systems to help solve increasing U.S. power demand and grid resilience challenges.
Solar-plus-storage scaling faces hurdles including software interoperability and supply chain constraints, as noted at RE+ 2025.
Stryten Energy's Summer 2024 internship program welcomed 25 interns, with 18 working at the Alpharetta, GA headquarters.
Mike Berger, Director of Product Engineering at Stryten Energy, discusses how safe, proven lead batteries contribute to clean energy transition.

Dan Autey, Executive Vice President of Commercial at Stryten Energy, explains that aging vehicles and expanding start-stop technology are increasing demand for advanced 12-volt batteries in the automotive aftermarket.
The company received AutoZone’s WITTDTJR Award on June 26, 2024, for demonstrating commitment to doing the job right.
Stryten Energy's M-Series Li600 LFP battery is designed for material handling fleets, offering longer cycle life and reliable lithium power.

The company sponsors teams for events like the Rebelle Rally and partners with BCI Flow Batteries North America to deliver battery-first technologies for commercial, industrial, and utility-scale applications.
Stryten Energy's Battle Born lithium solutions and Enhanced Flooded Battery technology were showcased at AAPEX 2025 to meet next-generation performance demands.
OEMs are increasingly shifting to EFB technology to meet rising power demands from start-stop systems, infotainment, and advanced safety features in modern vehicles.
Retailers can adapt to changing consumer needs by re-examining battery inventory mixes, as electrical loads rise with vehicle technology advancements.
Stryten Energy's vanadium redox flow battery prototype at the Solar Technology Acceleration Center in Aurora, CO, has dispatched 22 MWh of energy over 1,000 cycles since 2020.
VRFB applications extend beyond utility-scale storage to microgrids for neighborhoods/office parks and uninterruptible power systems for data centers and military bases.
The company's technology-agnostic approach positions it to leverage lead, lithium, and vanadium chemistries across diverse energy storage markets.
Stryten Energy's domestic manufacturing footprint includes facilities in Alpharetta, GA; Salina, KS; Manchester, IA; and Reno, NV, supporting a robust U.S. supply chain.
The company has been recognized for 50 years of manufacturing excellence at its Salina, Kansas plant by the Kansas House of Representatives.
Stryten Energy's mobile microgrid and BESS solutions are positioned to address grid disruptions, peak demand, and energy resilience needs across commercial and industrial sectors.
The company's strategic focus on long-duration energy storage (LDES) and battery-first models addresses critical infrastructure challenges driven by AI adoption and renewable integration.
Stryten Energy's inCOMMAND™ platform and Material Handling Battery Finder tool streamline fleet management and battery-to-truck matching for material handling operations.
The company's commitment to domestic supply chains is reinforced by partnerships, DOE funding, and legislative engagements with U.S. Senators and Representatives touring manufacturing facilities.
Stryten Energy's vanadium electrolyte supply chain strategy leverages the Inflation Reduction Act’s Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit to drive cost-competitive domestic production.
The company's battery testing lab in Alpharetta supports R&D across lead, lithium, and vanadium chemistries to advance next-generation energy storage solutions.
Stryten Energy's product portfolio expansion includes the M-Series X-7 charger, Li610 lithium-ion battery, E-Series AGM batteries, and Battle Born Batteries® brand distribution rights.
The company's workforce initiatives include summer internship programs at its Georgia headquarters and recognition as a Military Friendly Employer for supporting veterans.
Stryten Energy's supply chain resilience is highlighted by its closed-loop lead recycling system, critical mineral sourcing strategies, and domestic manufacturing capabilities.
Stryten Energy's BESS installations at Vicars Community Center and Georgia Tech demonstrate real-world applications of battery storage for community resilience and institutional carbon-neutral goals.
Stryten Energy's collaboration with Snapping Shoals EMC in Covington, GA, evaluates VRFB technology performance across varied usage needs to maximize impact for energy providers.
The company's strategic partnerships include KION North America, Dragonfly Energy, Storion Energy, and REVKIT to expand market reach and product accessibility.
Stryten Energy's focus on EV charging infrastructure support is evident through its BESS solutions designed for microgrids, data centers, and commercial/industrial applications.

The company's leadership team, including Scott Childers (VP Essential Power), Jeremy Furr (SVP Strategic Sourcing), Mike Berger (Director Product Engineering), and Dan Autey (EVP Commercial), drives innovation in battery technology and supply chain strategy.

facts: Georgia’s Central Region is a large, diverse area with manufacturing, agriculture and logistics businesses along with new opportunities in the film industry and in the rehabilitation of classic downtowns.
The region covers 25 counties and is convenient to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and the Port of Savannah.
Jasper County Industrial Park in Monticello is at 80 percent capacity with only five acres of available space.
Focus is shifting to the 50-acre Minetta Crossing site, which is being marketed as a green park, has railroad access and is drawing attention from prospects.
Thomas and Thwaite, a manufacturer of wood flooring, will open a 20-acre facility this spring and create more than 20 jobs.
Norton Packaging expanded and doubled its size and its workforce to 66 employees who produce rigid plastic pails and cans.
Water, Wind and Soil, which makes grass-like construction mats, has opened a facility and has 35 employees.
David Dyer, executive director of the Development Authority of Jasper County, says the area is courting 13 prospects that could result in 400-plus new jobs.
Downtown Monticello has its storefronts filled, so the city is looking for land close to town that will support microbusinesses in smaller commercial retail units.
Baldwin County purchased land for a new 1,643-acre Sibley-Smith Industrial Park, the state’s latest mega site.
The Sibley-Smith site has utilities, access to rail and is certified Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD).

Matt Poyner, executive director of the Development Authority of the City of Milledgeville and Baldwin County, says they started getting a lot of good activity for companies who want to move there and invest and grow once the land was bought.
Sparta Industries, a manufacturer of HVAC duct liner insulation, will establish a new plant at the former Rheem Manufacturing facility in Milledgeville.
The Sparta Industries venture is expected to create more than 1,000 jobs and represents an investment of $22.5 million.
Strapak Inc., a Japanese firm that manufactures plastic strapping used in packaging, has begun production in the building purchased from the county.
Strapak’s estimated $4-million to $6-million investment includes 10 new jobs.
Wilkinson County saw German-based chemical company Zschimmer and Schwarz invest $15 million in an expansion.
The Zschimmer and Schwarz project includes 75,000 square feet of laboratory, office and warehouse space on a 300-acre site and should create 60 jobs.
In Morgan County, luxury tile manufacturer Mannington Mills started the second phase of its expansion, which will add 50,000 square feet and produce 25 to 50 new jobs.
Bard Manufacturing, a national HVAC company, added 180,000 square feet and created 34 jobs last year.
Bob Hughes, president and economic development director for the Madison-Morgan County Chamber of Commerce, says adding 100 manufacturing jobs is a great increase.
Morgan County is constructing a $35-million hospital to replace the existing Morgan Memorial Hospital built in 1960.
The new hospital facility will be built on the U.S. 441 Bypass on a $1-million tract of donated land.

Pat Topping, senior vice president of the Macon Economic Development Commission, says more than a dozen companies expanded in 2016 in Macon-Bibb County, creating 600 jobs with an investment of $30 million.
Seven new companies arrived in Macon-Bibb County in 2016, representing a $40-million investment and 300-plus jobs.
Topping says manufacturing is hanging in there in the 8 to 9 percent range and inching up.
Ground has been broken for the Lofts at Capricorn, 137 apartments to be constructed adjacent to the old Capricorn Records recording studio in downtown Macon.
The Capricorn Records studio, where The Allman Brothers Band and other Southern rock bands recorded albums in the 1970s, has been donated to Mercer University.
The donated studio will be used as part of Mercer University’s music program and serve as a music incubator.
The Development Authority of Monroe County broke away from the local chamber of commerce and became an independent agency, hiring Kenneth “Bo” Gregory Jr. as president.
Michael Norris was brought on as economic development director for the city of Forsyth.
One51 plc, an Ireland-based molding company, acquired Encore Industries in Forsyth and will invest $18 million and create 20 jobs.
The Monroe County Fine Arts Center, a 1,200-seat auditorium, opened in 2016 at a cost of $8.2 million.
The auditorium will benefit Monroe County Schools’ performing arts programs and help attract performers from around the nation.
Laurens County welcomed the announced expansion of Valmiera Glass Co. in Dublin.
The Latvian fiberglass manufacturer has committed to an investment of $90 million through 2022.
The Valmiera Glass Co. project will create 425 new jobs.
Sun-Tek Pet, which produces and distributes dog and cat food, purchased two existing industrial facilities for a 40,000-square-foot expansion.
The Sun-Tek Pet $2.2-million investment will create 30 new jobs in Dublin.
Polymer Logistics, which produces plastic bins, crates and pallets, broke ground on a new 75,000-square-foot service center in Dublin.
Polymer Logistics is the sixth international business to locate in Laurens County and will employ 110 when it opens this year.
Macon County welcomed EnviroBuilders to Montezuma.
EnviroBuilders makes SmartSteel insulated panels for construction projects and could eventually employ as many as 100.
The county is in the process of funding a land bank capable of buying and rehabilitating rundown properties for resale.
A public-private partnership has created an enterprise zone to begin the revitalization of the downtown area.
The 200-acre Richland Solar Center was completed and opened in Twiggs County near Jeffersonville’s Georgia Hwy. 96.

Judith Sherling, executive director of the Twiggs County Development Authority, says the county is ideally positioned for future solar projects because it has multiple locations with transmission lines, a number of substations and flat land that is relatively inexpensive.
In Putnam County, Legacy Housing took over the manufactured home plant left vacant when Horton Homes closed.
Legacy was able to retain about 75 percent of the Horton workforce and will employ 140.
Eatonton could soon become a hub for the film industry.
Savannah-based Tytan Pictures has leased the old Putnam State Prison, which closed in 2014.
Tytan has also leased the town’s old aluminum plant and will transform it into a sound stage and postproduction offices.
The company has numerous projects planned for the new location, the first being an international action feature, King Seed: The Movie.
Gemi Trucking subsidiary Ocean Links purchased a warehouse to establish a logistics center in Eatonton.
Healthcare Plus added 30 new jobs in Eatonton, increasing its ranks to 70.
Husqvarna outdoor power product manufacturer broke ground for an expansion of its facility in Telfair County.
The new press operation will make the housing for items like mowers and snow blowers, create approximately 100 full-time jobs and 100 seasonal jobs.
Paula Rogers Anderson, president of the Telfair County Chamber of Commerce, says Husqvarna currently employs more than 1,000 people at the plant in McRae.
Telfair County’s renewed lease on the privately owned McRae Correctional Facility will allow 350 jobs to remain in the area.
Houston County saw the addition of $120 million in investments and 170 new jobs in 2016, says Angie Gheesling, executive director of the Houston County Development Authority.
German textile company Sandler AG announced it is expanding into Perry and bringing an additional 150 jobs.
Gheesling says this will open them up to more foreign investment and notes the industrial base is starting to grow.
Peach County Development Authority focused much of its effort on revamping its marketing and branding approach.
The county redesigned its website and changed its slogan to “We Grow Industry Sweet.”
Director B.J. Walker says the county is now in a better place to move forward with new partnerships and expansions.
Fort Valley’s Blue Bird Corp. added 200 employees for a new second shift at its school bus body assembly plant.
Production at the Blue Bird facility has increased by more than 50 percent in recent years.
Jones County continues to make incremental gains in its industrial park, says William Mathews, development authority director.
The Jones County site has received GRAD certification and will be aggressively marketed over the next year.
Ben Hill County’s industrial park received GRAD certification to make it more competitive for recruiting new business.
Shaw Industries closed its plant in Fitzgerald in 2016, but Jason Dunn, executive director of the Fitzgerald-Ben Hill Development Authority, says there has been interest in acquiring the property.
Manufacturing jobs are on the rise in Irwin County.
Forest River, which produces commercial trailers, added another line and hired 108 people, says Earl Denham, director of the Ocilla-Irwin Industrial Authority.
A sister company, Rockport Commercial Vehicles, also expanded.
Wheeler County is receiving a $22-million investment from the state to repurpose a former adult probation facility into a 56-bed regional youth detention center in Caldwell.
Construction on the Wheeler County detention center is expected to start by early 2018 and create 80 jobs.
Dooly County landed a pair of $100-million projects, says Bob Jeter, director of the Dooly County Economic Development Council.
Tyson Foods is upgrading its chicken processing line, adding 842 jobs.
Ensyn, which produces biofuel from forest and agricultural residues, is constructing a facility that will create 138 jobs.
As a whole, the region continues its economic expansion, delivering substantial job growth.
source_title: Central Georgia: Economic Muscle - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: FREYR Battery, Inc. is a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity.
The company is commencing development of the Giga America battery manufacturing project in Coweta County, Georgia, U.S.
Over the initial phase, Giga America is expected to generate hundreds of jobs in Coweta County, GA and surrounding areas.
FREYR’s Asset Mo team at the Customer Qualification Plant (“CQP”) conducted successful automated anode casting trials with active anode slurry in the second half of February 2024.
Cathode casting trials were successfully completed earlier in February 2024 at the CQP.
The remaining major milestone before expected start of automated production at the CQP is integration of casting webs across cathode, anode, and merge units using the Multi-Carrier System (“MCS”).
FREYR expects to commence production of functional battery cells for customer samples using full automation of the CQP in H1 2024.
CQP progress is being used to support a U.S. Department of Energy Title 17 application.
FREYR is positioning itself to qualify for a conditional commitment from the DOE Loan Programs Office by year-end 2024.
The DOE Title 17 application is tied to development of Giga America using the 24M Technologies (“24M”) U.S.-based SemiSolidTM platform.
FREYR launched the FREYR 2.0 growth initiative, encompassing pursuit of five major opportunities tied to distinct projects using either the SemiSolid platform or conventional technology.
The FREYR 2.0 initiative targets more than 100 GWh of production capacity in the U.S. and Europe.
Prospective end markets for the 2.0 initiative include Energy Storage Systems (“ESS”) and passenger EV applications, with possibility to produce both LFP and NMC cells.
The 2.0 initiative includes separate potential inorganic opportunities to accelerate path to market and first revenues.
Birger Steen serves as FREYR’s Chief Executive Officer.
On February 7th, 2024, FREYR published an operations update indicating automatic casting of electrodes with active electrolyte slurry in a dry room environment at the CQP in Mo i Rana, Norway.
Year-end 2023 cash balance was $275.7 million in cash, restricted cash and equivalents.
FREYR has a two-year cash runway before any new financings associated with the DOE Title 17 process and/or prospective project-level equity raise for Giga America.

2024 spending will focus on achieving production using full automation at CQP with 24M technology in H1 2024, delivering testable cells to customers, continued development of Giga America including costs for conditional credit approval in 2024 from DOE Title 17 loan program, and pursuing a conventional technology agreement.
On January 2nd, 2024, FREYR announced completion of redomicile from Luxembourg to the United States following a shareholder vote on December 15, 2023.

The Board believes redomiciling will enhance shareholder value by simplifying corporate structure and reporting requirements, expanding eligibility for equity indexes, aligning with NYSE listing standards and SEC governance requirements, and positioning FREYR to better respond to U.S. incentive programs for battery manufacturers.
As of publication, FREYR completed handovers of 363 of 388 (94%) discrete production line equipment commissioning and testing packages at the CQP.
The remaining 6% of commissioning packages will be finalized upon final handover of Casting & Unit Cell Assembly equipment later this year, expected after commencement of functional cell production.

Customer validation of the SemiSolid production process at giga scale and acceptance of product performance are key precursors to anticipated customer offtake conversions and unlocking potential project-level equity and debt financing for Giga America.
FREYR targets completion of the DOE Title 17 application process and securing a conditional commitment from the Loan Programs Office before year-end 2024.
FREYR targets formalization and announcement of an agreement with a conventional battery technology solutions provider potentially in H1 2024.
FREYR seeks to serve primary markets of ESS and commercial mobility, and maintains ambition to serve the passenger electric vehicles market.
Investor contact is Jeffrey Spittel, Head of Investor Relations.
Media contact is Amy Jaick, Global Head of Communications.

facts: EVs encompass road vehicles, rail, boats, submersibles, aircraft, and spacecraft.
Technological advances in lithium batteries since the late 20th century revived public interest as zero-emission vehicle options.
Plug-in hybrid electric vehicles did not see mass production until the late 2000s, and battery electric cars became practical for consumers in the 2010s.
As of 2026, China's EV industry produces more than the rest of the world combined.
Government incentives are available in many areas to promote EV adoption to reduce tailpipe emissions and fossil fuel dependency.
Over 20% of new cars sold in 2024 were electric only, while only 2% of trucks were.
China accounted for more than 70% of global EV production and 67% of global sales in 2024.
Most battery-electric vehicles use lithium-ion batteries due to higher energy density, longer lifespan, and higher power density compared to other practical batteries.
Nickel-metal hydride batteries were used historically but are outdated due to self-discharge in heat and a Chevron patent that hindered development.
Lithium-ion battery prices declined over the past decade, but critical mineral prices like lithium increased from 2021 to the end of 2022.
Most battery-electric vehicles include a separate low-voltage auxiliary battery (typically 12V) that powers accessories and control electronics, recharged via a DC/DC converter.
In 2024, Hyundai and Kia recalls cited failures of the Integrated Charging Control Unit (ICCU) that could stop charging the 12-volt battery and cause loss of drive power.
Electric motors achieve approximately 90% energy conversion efficiency over their full range of speeds and power output.
Tesla vehicles implement induction motors, while Nissan Leaf and Chevrolet Bolt use permanent magnet machines.
AC charging stations supply grid power to the vehicle's onboard charger (OBC), which converts it to DC for battery recharging.
DC chargers integrate the converter into the station to provide higher-power charging, bypassing the vehicle's onboard converter.

GM is adding V2H (bidirectional charging) capability to 2024 Silverado and Blazer EV models, allowing vehicles to send power to homes during grid outages, with promises to continue through model year 2026.
Battery swapping involves mechanically replacing batteries at special stations in minutes instead of recharging from sockets.
One source estimates that over a fifth of the lithium and about 65% of the cobalt needed for electric cars will come from recycled sources by 2035.

EVs release no tailpipe air pollutants and reduce respiratory illnesses like asthma, though their overall environmental impact depends on electricity generation sources and battery manufacturing/recycling impacts.
